title: Expose thin SDU and DRCP routing settings in profiles
description:
## Current gap

The current thin driver supports an explicit `ConnectOptions::with_sdu(...)`, and the local oraclemcp DB crate already has `DrcpConfig` for appending `server=pooled`, `pool_connection_class`, and `pool_purity` to EZConnect strings. The strict profile schema does not expose either as first-class profile config, so operators must hand-edit connect strings or cannot use the local `DrcpConfig` at all.

## Required change

Expose and wire supported thin network/session routing knobs from profiles:

- SDU request size mapped to `oracledb::ConnectOptions::with_sdu(...)`
- DRCP pooled-server settings using existing `oraclemcp_db::DrcpConfig`
- documented interaction when a connect string already includes query parameters such as `wallet_location`

## Design requirements

- Keep defaults unchanged: no DRCP unless explicitly configured, driver default SDU unless explicitly configured.
- Validate SDU ranges at config load or map through the driver's documented clamp with tests that make the behavior explicit.
- Do not log full connect strings after DRCP parameters are appended.
- Keep this separate from `[profiles.pool]`; DRCP is an Oracle server-routing feature, while the local pool is an oraclemcp client-side reuse feature.

## Acceptance criteria

- Config schema accepts documented DRCP and SDU fields.
- Inheritance works for DRCP and SDU fields.
- `profile_to_options` applies DRCP before building `OracleConnectOptions.connect_string`.
- `to_connect_options` applies SDU through the driver builder.
- Unit tests cover dedicated server default, DRCP with class/purity, existing connect-string query params, SDU default/override, and redaction.
- README profile examples explain when to prefer named fields over raw connect-string query parameters.
close_reason: Implemented typed profile SDU and DRCP routing fields with validation, inheritance, profile-to-driver mapping, redaction-safe diagnostics/docs, setup templates, and tests. Verified with git diff --check, cargo fmt --all -- --check, cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test --workspace, cargo deny check, plus calibrated single-bead compliance audit note.

Key coverage in the passing suite includes config DRCP/SDU parsing/validation, inheritance, redaction; core profile_connect_string/profile_to_options DRCP+SDU mapping with existing query params; DB to_connect_options with_sdu default/override; profile metadata/setup template redaction/docs. title: config: explicit ORACLEMCP_CONFIG (relative / missing / directory / empty) silently ignored — fail-closed validation
description:
CONFIRMED via code review + figment 0.10.19 behavior + live binary, then FIXED with tests. Discovered in the 2026-07 multipass bug hunt (Pass 1 config target).

BUG: OracleMcpConfig::default_config_path() (crates/oraclemcp-config/src/lib.rs) returned the raw ORACLEMCP_CONFIG value with no validation; figment's Toml::file() then maps an unresolvable path to File(None), so it silently loads an EMPTY config (defaults + env, ZERO profiles) with no diagnostic. Three concrete failure modes:
- F1 (security): a RELATIVE value makes figment walk cwd AND every ancestor dir for the filename, so the loaded profiles/credentials/ceilings depend on the launch directory.
- F2 (fail-closed): a missing path, a directory, or a path with trailing whitespace is SILENTLY ignored. An explicit operator pointer must be a hard, actionable error, not a silent boot with no profiles.
- F3: ORACLEMCP_CONFIG set to an exported-but-empty string loaded nothing AND suppressed the XDG / ~/.config discovery fallback.

FIX: added OracleMcpConfig::resolve_explicit_config_path() returning Result of Option PathBuf. It trims the value; empty becomes treated-as-unset (falls through to discovery, F3); non-absolute yields ConfigError::ExplicitConfigPathUnusable (F1); is_dir / not-is_file yields ExplicitConfigPathUnusable distinguishing directory-vs-missing (F2). figment() now returns Result and threads the error; load() propagates it. default_config_path() is now discovery-only (its explicit-env handling moved to the validated resolver) but still returns a valid explicit path for the infallible getter callers. Precedence preserved: CLI path beats validated ORACLEMCP_CONFIG beats XDG beats ~/.config.

VERIFIED non-findings from the audit, left intact: XDG relative-value handling already correct (config_search_dirs checks is_absolute); documented precedence matches code; no symlink/canonicalize issue.

TESTS: explicit_config_env_is_validated_fail_closed (missing errors 'no such file'; directory errors 'is a directory'; relative errors 'must be absolute'; trailing-whitespace trimmed then loads; empty falls back to ~/.config discovery); empty_xdg_is_ignored_and_empty_discovery_yields_none. HOME/XDG hard-unset is not unit-tested because edition-2024 std::env::remove_var is unsafe and this crate is forbid(unsafe_code); asserted the observable equivalent instead.

LIVE: serve --profile with ORACLEMCP_CONFIG at /tmp (dir), at /tmp/nope.toml (missing), at rel.toml (relative) each fail-closed at boot with the distinct actionable message. cargo test -p oraclemcp-config green (99 lib tests).
close_reason: Fixed: resolve_explicit_config_path validates ORACLEMCP_CONFIG (trim/empty to discovery; non-absolute/dir/missing to ConfigError); figment returns Result, load propagates; 2 tests; verified live on boot path; config suite green

title: Wire thin edition selection from profiles
description:
## Problem

oraclemcp already exposes `session_identity.edition` in profile config and carries it through `oraclemcp_core::profile_to_options`, but `oraclemcp_db::connection::apply_session_identity` still rejects any edition value with `UnsupportedFeature("edition-based redefinition selection is not supported by the published thin driver yet")`. The current pure-Rust thin driver now exposes `ConnectOptions::with_edition(...)` and applies it during authentication via `AUTH_ORA_EDITION`, so this is now an oraclemcp wiring gap rather than an upstream blocker.

## Required implementation

- Preserve the existing profile field name and inheritance behavior for `session_identity.edition`.
- Add `edition` to the `OracleConnectOptions` -> thin `ConnectOptions` bridge and call the driver edition option before connect/authentication.
- Remove the stale unsupported-feature rejection for edition from `apply_session_identity`.
- Keep edition values secret-safe in diagnostics and logs; do not echo arbitrary profile values in doctor output unless the tool is explicitly returning live Oracle session diagnostics.
- Preserve fail-closed SQL guard behavior and read-only operating-level ceilings.

## Acceptance criteria

- Unit tests prove profile edition config is mapped into `OracleConnectOptions` and then into the thin driver options.
- A regression test proves setting `session_identity.edition` no longer returns the stale unsupported-feature diagnostic before connect.
- Live Oracle 23ai/FREE validation instructions include checking `SYS_CONTEXT('USERENV','CURRENT_EDITION_NAME')` after connecting with a valid edition and verifying invalid/unauthorized editions fail at connect time with a structured server error.
- Docs, setup templates, and doctor/help text no longer describe thin edition selection as unsupported once this lands.

## Out of scope

- Creating or managing Oracle editions.
- Falling back silently to the database default edition when an explicit edition fails.
- Supporting non-thin/native thick mode.
notes: Unblocked: crates.io now has oracledb 0.2.1 (cargo search verified 2026-06-18), and local rust-oracledb 0.2.1 exposes ConnectOptions::with_edition. Proceed by updating oraclemcp from oracledb 0.1.1 to 0.2.1 and wiring session_identity.edition.
close_reason: Wired released thin edition selection via oracledb 0.2.1, added tests/docs/live validation, and recorded audit defense for deterministic false positive.

The old UnsupportedFeature rejection for edition was removed. Docs/setup guidance now describe edition as supported and include live validation instructions. Tests: cargo test -p oraclemcp-db thin_connect_options_apply_edition_when_configured -- --nocapture; cargo test -p oraclemcp-core session_identity_is_carried_to_connect_options -- --nocapture; cargo test -p oraclemcp-db --features live-xe live_edition_round_trip_when_configured -- --nocapture; cargo check --workspace; cargo test --workspace; cargo fmt --all -- --check; git diff --check; cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo deny check. Full live_oracle harness passes, but actual Oracle 23ai assertions still need valid ORACLEMCP_TEST_* credentials and ORACLEMCP_TEST_EDITION; current env returns ORA-01017.

title: [QA100][resolver] Answer SESSION_CONTEXT_SQL/SESSION_ROLES in offline test fakes
notes: codex's catalog_resolver (.82/.102/.103) added read_catalog_resolve_context which runs SESSION_CONTEXT_SQL (SELECT SYS_CONTEXT USERENV SESSION_USER/CURRENT_SCHEMA/CURRENT_EDITION_NAME FROM dual) and SESSION_ROLES_SQL, requiring SESSION_USER. The offline test fake OracleConnections in crates/oraclemcp/tests/{golden_behavior.rs,dashboard_e2e.rs,e2e_stdio.rs} implement query_rows by IGNORING the SQL and returning fixed rows with no SESSION_USER column, so read_catalog_resolve_context errors 'catalog resolver session user was missing' (CONNECTION_FAILED). 7 tests red: golden_behavior golden_stdio_* x3, dashboard_e2e x3, e2e_stdio x1. Pre-existing since the resolver work (verified reproduces on fde1498). FIX (offline): make each fake query_rows detect SESSION_CONTEXT_SQL (match on the SQL / SYS_CONTEXT) and return a row with SESSION_USER/CURRENT_SCHEMA/EDITION_NAME (use the mock's configured session_user e.g. APP), and answer SESSION_ROLES_SQL (empty or a role set), while returning the normal fixed rows for other queries. Regenerate goldens ONLY if the visible transcript legitimately changes to the resolver-success output (explain any diff; do not mask a real issue). Restores all-green (active goal).
close_reason: Answered the resolver dictionary queries in the offline test fakes. Deeper than just SESSION_CONTEXT_SQL: codex's res4 ensure_resolved_read_only runs a full semantic catalog resolution (session context + SESSION_ROLES + relation resolution vs ALL_OBJECTS/ALL_SYNONYMS + column resolution + purity vs ALL_POLICIES/ALL_TAB_COLS) before the caller's SQL. Added a shared resolver_dictionary_rows(sql,binds) helper to golden_behavior.rs + e2e_stdio.rs that branches on the resolver dictionary SQL and returns synthetic rows presenting the session as APP and every referenced relation as an ordinary VALID policy-free TABLE with each identifier a unique real column (bind-echoing so any name resolves), returning None for the caller's own statement so each mock's fixed rows pass through. Fixed 4 fakes (OneRowMock/PagedMock/ExportMock + SuccessfulQueryMock). NO goldens regenerated — the runtime reproduces the frozen pre-resolver success output exactly (correct: these plain SELECTs are proven read-only). The 4 resolver-caused tests are green: golden_behavior 9/0, e2e_stdio 9/0; fmt+clippy clean. NOTE: the 3 dashboard_e2e failures I originally lumped in are a SEPARATE misattributed issue (stale source-grep acceptance markers drifted by a route-spec refactor + title->input.title rename; features intact) — filed separately. Worktree-agent co-authored.

title: OCI IAM harness cannot enable required external authentication safely
description: Live revalidation 20260717T010506Z-2839264 proved the local patched driver reaches real ADB wallet TCPS (TLS established -> split CONNECT -> ACCEPT -> authenticated). Exact trace reached CONNECT reply: type=2 (ACCEPT); doctor reports connected + authenticated. OCI DB-token mint also succeeds, but the token was NOT presented to Oracle: harness stops before real_adb_tcps_signoff.sh because oracle_preview_sql correctly refuses BEGIN DBMS_CLOUD_ADMIN.ENABLE_EXTERNAL_AUTHENTICATION('OCI_IAM'); END;. Therefore this is not merely a verification-SELECT refusal and token acceptance remains unproven. Keep generic oracle_execute fail-closed. Implement a safe harness/provisioning approach that enables the one-time ADB IAM prerequisite without generic caller PL/SQL bypass (or a tightly scoped audited/confirmed server surface if justified), then make the final IAM probe use a guard-proven READ_ONLY query and prove actual token authentication live. Acceptance: arbitrary caller PL/SQL remains refused; wallet and IAM final probes both run; IAM doctor/probe output proves token accepted; ADB is destroyed.
close_reason: Split direct ADMIN wallet bootstrap from the governed IAM probe; wallet TCPS and guarded SELECT USER prove harness path. Live IAM token CONNECT now reaches a distinct driver fast-auth capability blocker tracked by oraclemcp-he7t.

title: OCI live Autonomous DB (ADB) acceptance harness
description:
OCI/ADB testing IS achievable at ZERO license cost via Oracle Cloud ALWAYS FREE Autonomous Database (provisioned License-Included at no charge). Corrects the earlier "needs a licensed cloud tenancy" assumption. Two phases:

PHASE 1 — offline/autonomous (no cloud account; agents do this now): a synthetic-wallet + local rustls TCPS lane exercising the OCI code paths without any endpoint — cwallet.sso/3DES wallet parse, TCPS handshake, ssl_server_dn_match / SNI, and IAM database-token injection (token refused over non-TCPS). Covers the driver wallet/TCPS/TokenSource code and server plumbing. Verify what already exists (PLAN_0_7_3 designed this) and fill gaps.

PHASE 2 — real end-to-end vs Always Free ADB (needs a ONE-TIME free OCI account + API creds; then automatable): Terraform provisions oci_database_autonomous_database (is_free_tier=true) + oci_database_autonomous_database_wallet (base64_encode_content=true), downloads the wallet, suite runs mTLS wallet connect (port 1522), optional TLS-without-wallet (port 1521 + ACL), IAM database-token auth, and a real query over TCPS. Runs in CI with OCI creds as secrets OR as an operator gate; tear down after. This is the piece gvenzl cannot provide (no TCPS/wallet).

OPERATOR PREREQUISITE (phase 2 only): free OCI account (Always Free; card for identity only, never charged) + a CI service key/dynamic group. Not a license. ACCEPTANCE: phase 1 green autonomously in CI; phase 2 is tri-state (PASS on a real Always-Free ADB, or typed SKIP-blocked-on-OCI-creds until the account exists) — never a silent skip. all-versions-green is truthful only when 5c7i AND this OCI lane are both green.
notes:
Secrets: 6 OCI identity secrets SET in MuhDur/oraclemcp (2026-07-16). ADB_ADMIN_PASSWORD / ADB_WALLET_PASSWORD intentionally NOT operator-set — the Terraform generates Oracle-compliant random passwords (12-30 chars, upper+lower+digit, no "admin"/double-quote), used only for the throwaway ADB + wallet and torn down with it. REPO SPLIT: Phase-1 offline synthetic-wallet + local-rustls-TCPS lane exercises the DRIVER (rust-oracledb) wallet/TCPS/TokenSource code with NO secrets and can run in the driver CI as normal tests; Phase-2 real Always-Free-ADB E2E runs in oraclemcp CI (this bead) using the 6 secrets. Driver does NOT need the OCI secrets now. A driver-level real-ADB release gate is a FUTURE option — enable via an org-level secret shared to both repos (or duplicate), and stagger schedules because Always Free allows only 2 ADB instances per tenancy.

title: Zero-config consent-gated TNS onboarding for oraclemcp
description:
EPIC. Ship a consent-gated, zero-config onboarding path so a fresh user or agent is running READ_ONLY against their real databases within minutes, and can refine the config later by hand, by agent, or via the dashboard.

USER GOAL (distilled). During install and via the binary (oraclemcp setup --discover, an init flow), AFTER explicitly asking the human or agent for consent, search the system for tnsnames.ora, pull the net-service connection info, and AUTO-GENERATE the standard oraclemcp config the way oraclemcp needs it: every discovered net-service becomes a READ_ONLY profile with credentials as EXTERNAL secret refs (never literal secrets) and safe defaults. Write a general SAFE annotated config with ALL fields present, optional ones as COMMENTED lines carrying one-line help, bootable (passes serve and doctor). The result must round-trip through the same config-ops backend the dashboard and agents use, be idempotent and non-destructive (never clobber an existing config), and start the user ASAP.

WHAT ALREADY EXISTS (investigated ground truth, cite in child beads).
- setup command and setup --write today: crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:209 (Setup subcommand args), run_setup at main.rs:3299, setup_apply_config_with_backend at main.rs:3205 (stage then apply then status through ConfigOpsService). The IX8 work made setup --write emit a MINIMAL bootable config via robot_docs::setup_profiles_template (crates/oraclemcp/src/robot_docs.rs:7). This epic EXTENDS that minimal path with discovery and an annotated writer.
- Config-ops backend (the safe writer): crates/oraclemcp-core/src/config_ops.rs. ConfigOpsService::stage / apply / status / rollback. It validates drafts with the strict loader (OracleMcpConfig::from_toml_str), writes a timestamped verbatim backup (backup_path_for at config_ops.rs:655), then same-directory write-temp-and-rename under the service lock (write_atomic_path at config_ops.rs:680), revalidates the installed file, and exposes only redacted metadata. Because the draft bytes are written VERBATIM, TOML comments survive a round-trip, which is what makes an annotated file compatible.
- Profile schema and all fields: crates/oraclemcp-config/src/profile.rs:457 (ConnectionProfile). Accessors: max_level and default_level both default to READ_ONLY (profile.rs:588 and profile.rs:595), protected default false (profile.rs:601), mcp_exposed default true (profile.rs:619). connect_string accepts EZConnect, EZConnect-Plus, or a tnsnames.ora alias (profile.rs:463). Canonical annotated reference: oraclemcp.example.toml (every field with an inline one-line help comment), kept honest by crates/oraclemcp-config/tests/example_config_parses.rs.
- TNS parser to REUSE (do not hand-roll one): oracledb re-exports oracledb-protocol as oracledb::protocol (oracledb-0.5.1 src/lib.rs:184). The parser is oracledb::protocol::net::connectstring::tnsnames::TnsnamesReader (source: oracledb-protocol-0.5.1 src/net/connectstring/tnsnames.rs:15). It reads tnsnames.ora from a config dir, follows IFILE includes, detects include cycles, splits comma-separated alias lists, upper-cases aliases, last-definition-wins, and exposes read(dir), service_names(), get(alias), file_name(). It is fuzzed upstream. Reuse means correct IFILE and edge handling for free.
- Driver seam constraint: every oracledb:: path is quarantined to crates/oraclemcp-db/src/connection.rs by scripts/oraclemcp_driver_seam_lint.sh (DRIVER_PATTERN at line 38) plus an in-crate test. The pattern matches oracledb followed by :: and does NOT match oracledb_protocol:: (underscore breaks it). Design consequence: the TNS parse wrapper either lives in oraclemcp-db behind the allowlist, or depends on the pinned oracledb-protocol crate directly. Child beads resolve this.
- Secret refs: crates/oraclemcp-auth/src/secrets.rs. Schemes env: file: keyring: vault: literal:. resolve_secret_with at secrets.rs:172; literal is rejected on protected profiles (secrets.rs:179 and the test at secrets.rs:370). Generated profiles MUST use env: refs, never literal.
- Dashboard and operator edits flow through the same backend: crates/oraclemcp-core/src/operator_protocol.rs routes /operator/v1/config, /config/draft, /config/apply, /config/rollback (operator_protocol.rs:100). These call ConfigOpsService. So config-ops compatibility is the round-trip guarantee.
- Installer: install.sh:595 print_next_steps already suggests setup --write and setup, and maybe_run_doctor at install.sh:604. install.ps1 parity at install.ps1:747. This epic wires discovery into that with explicit consent.
- Default config target: operator_config_target_path at main.rs:2198, default_config_path at crates/oraclemcp-config/src/lib.rs:485 (ORACLEMCP_CONFIG then ~/.config/oraclemcp/profiles.toml then config.toml).
- Safety invariant (AGENTS.md:42): fail-closed guard, READ_ONLY default and cap, protected pinned READ_ONLY, pure Rust, forbid unsafe. All generated config must honor this.

ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS (rationale in child beads).
1. Discovery lives in pure Rust in the binary, not in bash: testable, cross-platform, and it reuses the exact config-ops writer, so install.sh and install.ps1 only invoke it.
2. Consent is mandatory and explicit: TTY prompt for humans, an explicit flag for agents, CI, and non-TTY; never scan-and-write without consent.
3. Reuse TnsnamesReader; never ship a bespoke TNS parser.
4. Every discovered net-service becomes a READ_ONLY, env-ref, non-protected profile; unverifiable profiles stay READ_ONLY (fail closed).
5. The annotated writer is the source-of-truth-reconciled bridge between the minimal starter and oraclemcp.example.toml.

ACCEPTANCE (epic-level). A clean machine with a synthetic TNS_ADMIN dir can run one consented command and end up with a bootable annotated profiles.toml (serve starts, doctor passes offline), one READ_ONLY env-ref profile per net-service, a backup of any pre-existing file, and no secrets on disk; re-running does not clobber; the dashboard config apply endpoint accepts the same file unchanged. Child beads carry the granular DoD and tests. All child work honors AGENTS.md:42 and the driver seam.
close_reason: COMPLETE: all 20 children (.1-.20) implemented, gated, and committed — zero-config consent-gated TNS onboarding shipped. Discovery/parse (seam-safe oracledb_protocol), synth/writer (READ_ONLY, env: refs, config-ops round-trip), full setup --discover flow (consent exit-2 refusal, idempotent add-only merge, agent JSON, doctor), installer parity, tests (unit+edge+consent+e2e), docs. Full gate green (build + 1338 tests [only pre-existing pty/pwsh env failure] + clippy 0 + fmt + all 8 lints + deny + no cycles). Independent fresh-eyes security audit: Critical=0; the one Important (I1 fallback-legibility) + one Nit (N1 doc) fixed. Safety invariants all verified: consent-before-scan, no-secrets-on-disk, READ_ONLY, config-ops verify-before-mutate, driver-seam, forbid(unsafe).

title: Discovery and mapping design spec (anchor contract)
description:
DESIGN ANCHOR. Author the single shared spec the implementation beads build on, so no implementer needs the originating conversation. Cite exact anchors; keep this reconciled with oraclemcp.example.toml.

Deliver a spec (a doc section under docs/ and mirrored in code doc-comments) covering:

A. SEARCH ORDER for tnsnames.ora candidate directories, first-match-wins but scan-all-for-report: TNS_ADMIN env, ORACLE_HOME/network/admin, ~/.config/oraclemcp/network (the wrapper default set by setup_wrapper_template at crates/oraclemcp/src/robot_docs.rs:22, ORACLE_NET_HOME line at robot_docs.rs:26), ~/, /etc, common Instant Client dirs, and the current directory. De-duplicate by canonical path. Permission-denied on a candidate is a skip-with-note, never a hard failure.

B. NET-SERVICE to PROFILE mapping rules: naming, default_profile choice, connect_string vs alias, credential_ref placeholder, READ_ONLY defaults, protected disposition, mcp_exposed disposition. Reference the full ConnectionProfile field set at crates/oraclemcp-config/src/profile.rs:457 to profile.rs:550.

C. ANNOTATED WRITER contract. The writer renders a bootable minimum plus a self-documenting commented menu. Fix the disposition of EVERY field.
- TOP LEVEL (OracleMcpConfig at crates/oraclemcp-config/src/lib.rs:83; the struct is deny_unknown_fields, so every UNCOMMENTED key MUST be a real serde field and no unknown keys may appear): SET schema_version=2 and default_profile (when unambiguous); render monitor_profile as a commented one-line help (matches oraclemcp.example.toml:35); for the optional [http] and [audit] top-level sections do NOT reproduce the full surface (that duplicates oraclemcp.example.toml and the docs bead forbids duplication) — emit a short commented pointer to oraclemcp.example.toml, plus one safety note that [audit] key_ref must be configured before any profile max_level is raised above READ_ONLY or startup fails closed (AuditConfig at lib.rs; fail-closed-without-key behavior).
- PER PROFILE (the 25 ConnectionProfile serde fields at profile.rs:457 to profile.rs:550): SET name, description, connect_string, credential_ref (env: placeholder), max_level=READ_ONLY, default_level=READ_ONLY (SET explicitly so the safety ceiling is legible and never relies on a default). SET username only when a least-privilege convention is known, else comment it. All remaining optional fields present but COMMENTED, each with a one-line help string using its EXACT serde name so uncommenting yields a valid key: login_script, login_statements, trusted_session_statements, call_timeout_seconds, connect_timeout_seconds, sdu, protected, require_signed_tools, read_only_standby, mcp_exposed, dashboard_ddl_workbench, [profiles.session_identity], [profiles.pool], [profiles.oci], [profiles.drcp], [profiles.proxy_auth], [[profiles.app_context]], base. Help strings are reconciled in MEANING with oraclemcp.example.toml (the example may SET some of these for illustration; the discovery writer comments them — reconciliation is on the help wording, not on set/unset disposition). Output MUST parse through OracleMcpConfig::from_toml_str (config-ops re-parses it at crates/oraclemcp-core/src/config_ops.rs:280).

D. CONSENT MATRIX: human TTY, agent, CI, non-TTY, and the exact flags and prompt wording. Non-TTY refusal exits with code 2 (usage_config_or_safety_block, per robot_docs cli_exit_codes_json).

E. IDEMPOTENCY and BACKUP contract: never clobber; existing-config detection; add-only-new merge; use the config-ops timestamped backup (backup_path_for at config_ops.rs:655) and its verify-before-mutate posture (CurrentChanged at config_ops.rs:311 and config_ops.rs:423).

F. CANONICAL TEST FIXTURE contract. Define ONE shared synthetic fixture tree that all discovery test beads reuse, so fixtures never diverge. It is created with the parse-adapter bead (.3) under a committed tests/fixtures/tns/ path co-located with the parse-adapter tests; the edge-case (.16), unit (.17), and e2e (.18) beads REUSE it, adding the extra variants below as sibling files in the SAME tree. Contents: a primary tnsnames.ora with multiple aliases including one TCPS descriptor with wallet_location, one EZConnect-style descriptor, one alias defined twice (duplicate, last-definition-wins), and an IFILE include of a second committed file that contributes its own aliases; plus sibling fixtures for a malformed/partly-broken tnsnames.ora, an IFILE include cycle, and a directory used to simulate permission-denied. Enumerate exact expected service_names and hint extractions so tests assert against fixed values.

RATIONALE. One spec bead keeps the mapping and writer contracts consistent across the parser, synth, writer, consent, and installer beads, keeps the fixture single-sourced, and gives fresh-eyes reviewers a single source of truth.

ACCEPTANCE. The spec enumerates every ConnectionProfile field (profile.rs:457 to profile.rs:550) AND every top-level OracleMcpConfig field (lib.rs:83) with its required-vs-commented-vs-pointer disposition and one-line help; states the deny_unknown_fields constraint; enumerates the search dirs and precedence; states the consent matrix with exit code 2 and the exact non-TTY refusal and success wording; states the merge, ask, and backup rules; defines the canonical fixture tree. It contradicts nothing in the AGENTS.md safety invariant.

TEST PLAN. This is a design bead; verification is a review checklist embedded in the doc plus a stub test asserting the field-disposition table lists every serde field name of ConnectionProfile AND OracleMcpConfig (guards against schema drift; complements the anti-rot test in bead .9).
close_reason: Design anchor authored: docs/tns-discovery-onboarding.md (search order §A, mapping §B, per-field writer contract §C for all 25 ConnectionProfile + 6 OracleMcpConfig serde fields, consent matrix §D w/ exit-2 + exact wording, idempotency/backup §E, canonical fixture tree §F w/ enumerated values). Mirrored in oraclemcp-config::discovery::contract with FieldDisposition tables + schema-drift tests (green).

title: Wire the setup --discover / init orchestration
description:
TASK. Add the command surface that orchestrates the whole flow: resolve search dirs, obtain consent, parse net-services, synthesize READ_ONLY env-ref profiles, render the annotated config, and apply it through the existing config-ops backend exactly as setup --write does today.

BACKGROUND. The setup subcommand args are declared at crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:209 (the new --discover, --discover-tns, --yes, and --dry-run flags attach here alongside the existing --write and --json). Todays setup --write path: run_setup at main.rs:3299 builds the payload then calls setup_apply_config_with_backend at main.rs:3205, which does ConfigOpsService::stage then apply then status (ConfigOpsService at crates/oraclemcp-core/src/config_ops.rs:362). The target path is operator_config_target_path at main.rs:2198. Extend this, do not fork it.

DESIGN and RATIONALE. Add setup --discover (and an init alias if warranted) that: (1) runs the search resolver and parse adapter; (2) runs the consent gate; (3) on zero net-services found, FALLS BACK to the minimal starter (setup_profiles_template at robot_docs.rs:7) so the user is never left empty-handed; (4) on one or many, synthesizes profiles and renders the annotated config; (5) supports --dry-run, which prints the report and the would-write config and writes NOTHING (this is the flow the idempotency bead .11 and the JSON bead .13 build their previews on); (6) otherwise writes through ConfigOpsService::stage then apply (reusing setup_apply_config_with_backend so backup, atomic replace, revalidation, and reload plan all come for free); (7) prints the report and next actions. Reusing config-ops is the design choice: it gives the timestamped backup (backup_path_for at config_ops.rs:655), the same-directory atomic rename (write_atomic_path at config_ops.rs:680), strict revalidation, and the reload plan the live service and dashboard already consume, with no new write path to audit.

ACCEPTANCE. setup --discover with consent on a machine with a fixture TNS_ADMIN produces a bootable annotated config at the target path via config-ops, with a backup when a file pre-existed; --dry-run writes nothing; zero-found falls back to the minimal starter; the command emits a report of found net-services, chosen profile names, and env vars to set. Errors map to the existing setup error codes (setup_config_error_status at main.rs:3255). Honors the AGENTS.md safety invariant and the driver seam.

TEST PLAN. Integration test driving the command against a fixture TNS_ADMIN and a temp target: assert file written through config-ops (backup present), report contents, and offline doctor pass. Zero-found fallback and --dry-run no-write covered. See crates/oraclemcp/tests/cli.rs and clean_machine_e2e.rs.
close_reason: Wired setup --discover orchestration: resolve candidate dirs, parse tnsnames, bridge to DiscoveredNetService, synthesize READ_ONLY profiles, render annotated config, write through config-ops; --dry-run previews; zero-found falls back to minimal starter; spec D success line on stderr; CLI tests green

title: Idempotent, non-destructive merge and backup
description:
TASK. Make re-running discovery safe: never clobber an existing config, detect what is already there, and merge, ask, or back up rather than overwrite edited content.

BACKGROUND. config-ops already writes a verbatim timestamped backup before any replace (backup_path_for at crates/oraclemcp-core/src/config_ops.rs:655) and refuses to apply if the current file changed under it (CurrentChanged at config_ops.rs:311 and, on the service path, config_ops.rs:423). That gives verify-before-mutate; this bead adds the merge and ask policy on top.

DESIGN and RATIONALE. On re-run, load the current target (operator_config_target_path at main.rs:2198). If it is absent or empty, write the generated config. If it already defines profiles: do NOT overwrite. Instead compute the set difference by deterministic profile name (from the synthesis bead) and offer to ADD only the net-services not already present, appending only new [[profiles]] blocks and leaving every existing profile, comment, and hand-edit byte-untouched. The merged bytes are re-parsed through OracleMcpConfig::from_toml_str BEFORE apply, so a merge that would produce an invalid config is rejected and nothing is written. In interactive mode, ask before writing the merged result; in --yes mode, add-only-new is the safe default and never mutates an existing profile block. Any write still goes through config-ops so a backup is captured and the current-hash guard prevents a racy clobber. Provide a --dry-run (from the orchestration bead .10) that prints the merge plan and writes nothing. The idempotency guarantee: running twice with the same inputs and no manual edits results in no change on the second run (the diff is empty, nothing written).

ACCEPTANCE. Second identical run makes no change and writes nothing. A run after the user added a net-service to tnsnames.ora adds only that new profile and preserves all existing content and comments. An existing hand-edited profile is never overwritten. A merge that would be invalid is rejected before any write. Every mutating write leaves a timestamped backup. A concurrent external edit is rejected (CurrentChanged) rather than clobbering. Honors the non-destructive requirement.

TEST PLAN. Integration tests: run twice assert no-op second run; pre-seed a config with an edited profile then discover assert edit preserved and only new profiles added; assert a deliberately-invalid merge is rejected with nothing written; assert backup artifact exists on mutate; simulate a racing write assert CurrentChanged. No live DB.
close_reason: Locked idempotent add-only merge: second identical run is a no-op writing nothing; merge preserves existing profiles/comments and adds only new by deterministic name; invalid existing config rejected before any write; timestamped backup on mutate; verify-before-mutate rejects a racing edit (CurrentChanged); zero-found minimal-starter fallback. CLI + core tests green

title: Config-ops and dashboard round-trip guarantee
description:
TASK. Guarantee the generated annotated config round-trips through the config-ops backend and the dashboard operator config endpoints unchanged, so later edits by agent, human, or dashboard use the same path.

BACKGROUND. The dashboard and operator edits flow through crates/oraclemcp-core/src/operator_protocol.rs routes /operator/v1/config, /config/draft, /config/apply, /config/rollback (operator_protocol.rs:100), which call ConfigOpsService (config_ops.rs:362). Draft bytes are written verbatim (config_ops.rs:680) and re-parsed strictly (config_ops.rs:280), and status is redacted (config_ops.rs redacted_snapshot).

DESIGN and RATIONALE. Because config-ops writes verbatim and validates strictly, an annotated file with comments is a valid draft: submitting it to /config/draft then /config/apply must produce a byte-identical (or comment-preserving) result and a clean redacted status listing the discovered profiles. This bead adds the tests that lock that contract so the annotated writer cannot emit something the dashboard cannot round-trip (for example a construct the strict loader accepts but redaction or reload-plan diffing chokes on).

ACCEPTANCE. Feeding the generated config to stage then apply yields a valid apply outcome and a redacted status enumerating exactly the generated profiles with no secret leakage; a subsequent no-op re-apply of the same bytes is stable; the reload plan between empty and generated is computed without error. No secret material appears in any preview, status, or diff.

TEST PLAN. Integration test in the core crate exercising ConfigOpsService::stage, apply, status against the generated annotated bytes; assert redacted status profile set and zero secret leakage (mirror the leakage assertions in config_ops.rs tests at config_ops.rs:769). No live DB.
close_reason: Locked config-ops/dashboard round-trip: generated annotated config stages+applies through ConfigOpsService verbatim (comments preserved), status enumerates exactly the generated READ_ONLY profiles, no-op re-apply is stable, empty-to-generated reload plan computes cleanly, and no secret material leaks into any redacted preview/status/diff (canary assertions)

title: Agent and non-TTY structured JSON path
description:
TASK. Provide a fully non-interactive, machine-readable discovery path for agents and CI, emitting a structured report, honoring the explicit-consent flag, and registering the command in the agent-facing CLI contract.

BACKGROUND. The binary already has a --json (alias of --robot-json) robot mode and structured setup payloads (setup_payload at crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:3107; setup_write_payload at main.rs:3221). The agent-facing CLI contract is emitted by cli_contract_json (crates/oraclemcp/src/robot_docs.rs), which lists read_side_json_commands and dangerous_operations with safe_preview and execute_gate. The exit-code taxonomy is cli_exit_codes_json (robot_docs.rs); code 2 is usage_config_or_safety_block. Agents drive the CLI headless.

DESIGN and RATIONALE. With --json plus the consent flag (--yes or --discover-tns from the consent bead), setup --discover emits a JSON document: the searched directories and per-dir notes, the found net-services, the chosen profile names, the connect_string strategy per profile, the env vars the agent must set, whether a backup was written and where, the target path, and the ordered next actions (set env vars, run doctor, run doctor --online). Without a consent flag in non-TTY mode it emits a structured refusal (from the consent bead) naming the flag and exits 2 (usage_config_or_safety_block). Redaction: the JSON never contains secret values, only env-var NAMES and safe metadata, matching setup --write (main.rs:3237). Because discovery WRITES config, register it in cli_contract_json under dangerous_operations with safe_preview (the --dry-run or --json-without-consent form) and execute_gate (--yes or --discover-tns), and add the --json form so an agent that has never seen this feature discovers it from the contract surface. This makes the flow scriptable and self-describing.

ACCEPTANCE. setup --discover --yes --json returns valid JSON with the fields above and exit 0; setup --discover --json in non-TTY without consent returns a structured refusal and exit 2; no secret values in the JSON; the next-actions list is executable verbatim; cli_contract_json lists setup --discover under dangerous_operations with its safe_preview and consent execute_gate.

TEST PLAN. CLI tests asserting JSON shape and required keys, the refusal shape and exit 2, absence of secret material, and that the contract JSON now advertises setup --discover. See crates/oraclemcp/tests/cli.rs.
close_reason: Agent JSON path: setup --discover --json emits a complete structured report (searched dirs, net-services, per-profile connect-string strategy, env-var names, profiles created/skipped, target + backup path, ordered executable next_actions) with names only and no secret values; non-TTY without consent emits a structured refusal exit 2; registered setup --discover in cli_contract_json dangerous_operations (safe_preview=dry-run, execute_gate=consent flags). CLI tests green

title: Doctor validation of the discovered config
description:
TASK. Ensure the discovered config passes doctor offline and that doctor surfaces actionable, non-blocking hints for each discovered profile that still needs a credential or verification. Also update the no-profiles-configured guidance to advertise discovery as the zero-config fast path.

BACKGROUND. doctor loads a profile and runs offline checks by default, online with --online (Doctor subcommand at crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:160; DoctorContext assembled at main.rs:4749; tns_admin surfaced at main.rs:4752). When no profiles are configured, doctor already guides the user to setup --write and to export the credential env var (guidance in the main.rs:4602 region, which today names only setup --write and ORACLE_APP_PASSWORD).

DESIGN and RATIONALE. After discovery writes the config, oraclemcp doctor (offline) must report no config blocker: the annotated file loads, profiles are READ_ONLY, TNS_ADMIN is reported if set. For each profile whose credential env var is unset, doctor emits a non-blocking hint (export ORACLE_SALES_RO_PASSWORD, then run doctor --online --profile sales_ro) rather than a blocker, so the user can boot immediately and fill credentials incrementally; the hint uses the exact per-profile env-var name from the secret-ref bead (.6). A profile that cannot be verified stays READ_ONLY (fail closed, per the read-only-safe-defaults bead .7) and doctor marks it needs-verification. Additionally, update the no-profiles-configured guidance (main.rs:4602 region) to also suggest oraclemcp setup --discover as the zero-config fast path alongside setup --write, so doctor guides a fresh user straight into discovery. Reuse the existing doctor plumbing; do not add a parallel validator.

ACCEPTANCE. doctor offline on a freshly discovered config exits 0 with no blocker; per-profile credential-missing hints are present and name the exact env var; TNS_ADMIN is echoed when set; an unverifiable profile is reported needs-verification and remains READ_ONLY; the no-profiles guidance mentions setup --discover. No secret values printed.

TEST PLAN. Integration test: discover against a fixture, run doctor offline, assert exit 0 and the presence of the per-profile hints and TNS_ADMIN echo; a separate assertion that the empty-config doctor guidance now names setup --discover. Online verification against a real DB is covered by the e2e bead only where a live target exists; otherwise offline. See crates/oraclemcp/tests/cli.rs.
close_reason: Doctor validates discovered config: offline doctor exits 0 (no blocker); per-profile non-blocking credential hint names the exact env var + doctor --online --profile <name> command when the env: credential is unset (fail-closed needs-verification, profile stays READ_ONLY); TNS_ADMIN reported via the TNS/wallet check; no-profiles guidance now advertises setup --discover as the zero-config fast path (main.rs + doctor.rs connectivity_fix). No secret values printed. CLI + doctor tests green

title: Installer integration (install.sh and install.ps1)
description:
TASK. Wire the pure-Rust discovery into the installers so an interactive install can offer to discover and write profiles, while non-interactive installs never scan without an explicit flag.

BACKGROUND. install.sh print_next_steps already suggests setup --write and setup (install.sh:595) and maybe_run_doctor gates on interactivity plus a yes flag (install.sh:604). install.ps1 has parity: next steps at install.ps1:746 and Invoke-OptionalDoctor at install.ps1:711. Existing installer tests live in crates/oraclemcp/tests/installer_e2e.rs. The install-oneliner UX shipped in 0.6.2; this is the follow-on.

DESIGN and RATIONALE. The installers must NOT reimplement discovery in bash or PowerShell; they invoke the binary (oraclemcp setup --discover) which carries the consent gate, the config-ops write, and the report. Interactive install: after the binary is in place, offer Discover databases from tnsnames.ora now (defaulting to No), and on Yes run oraclemcp setup --discover (interactive consent inside the binary). Non-interactive install (piped, no TTY, or without the installer yes flag): do NOT scan; only print the next-step command (oraclemcp setup --discover) so the user runs it deliberately. This keeps one source of truth (the binary) and keeps the installers thin, testable, and cross-platform-consistent.

ACCEPTANCE. Interactive install offers discovery, defaults No, and on Yes invokes the binary discovery which itself asks consent and writes via config-ops. Non-interactive install never scans and prints the discover next-step. install.sh and install.ps1 stay in parity. No discovery logic is duplicated in the shell.

TEST PLAN. Shellcheck plus a scripted non-interactive run asserting no scan and the printed next-step; a scripted interactive run (expect-style) asserting the prompt and the binary invocation. PowerShell parity check. Extend the installer test patterns in crates/oraclemcp/tests/installer_e2e.rs.
close_reason: install.sh maybe_offer_discovery + install.ps1 Invoke-OptionalDiscovery: interactive-only offer (default No) that delegates to 'oraclemcp setup --discover' (binary carries the consent gate + config-ops write); non-interactive never scans and print_next_steps/Write-NextStep advertise the command. No discovery logic duplicated in shell. New installer_e2e test installers_offer_consent_gated_tns_discovery_via_the_binary (source-string parity, PASS); smoke pty harness updated to feed the new prompt + assert it. Shellcheck-clean (SC2016 fixed), bash -n OK. installer_lint_and_offline_smoke_passes remains the pre-existing env failure (pty script -qefc unavailable in sandbox — verified identical with changes stashed).

title: Edge-case handling and tests
description:
TEST plus hardening. Cover the messy real-world tnsnames.ora and filesystem conditions so discovery degrades gracefully and never produces an invalid or unsafe config. Reuse the CANONICAL fixture tree defined by the design spec (bead .1 item F) and owned by the parse-adapter bead (.3); add the malformed, include-cycle, and permission-denied variants as sibling files in the same tests/fixtures/tns/ tree so fixtures never diverge.

CASES (each an assertion, referencing the parser at oracledb-protocol-0.5.1 src/net/connectstring/tnsnames.rs:15 and the synthesis and writer beads).
- Malformed tnsnames.ora: a syntactically broken file yields the entries the parser can recover plus a note, never a panic or an invalid profile.
- Duplicate service names: last-definition-wins in the parser is respected; synthesis produces one profile with a deterministic name and notes the duplicate.
- Wallet or TCPS descriptors: a tcps descriptor with wallet_location produces a profile plus a commented [profiles.oci] wallet_password_ref env placeholder (secret-ref bead) and keeps READ_ONLY.
- IFILE includes: an included file contributes its net-services; an include cycle is reported as a structured error, not a hang.
- No tnsnames at all: falls back to the minimal starter (setup_profiles_template at crates/oraclemcp/src/robot_docs.rs:7) so the user still boots.
- Permission-denied reads: an unreadable candidate dir or file is skipped with a note; discovery continues.

ACCEPTANCE. Each case has a fixture and an assertion; none panics; every produced config parses via OracleMcpConfig::from_toml_str and is READ_ONLY; the no-tnsnames path yields the minimal starter.

TEST PLAN. Fixture-driven unit and integration tests colocated with the parser and init-flow tests, all reading the shared canonical fixture tree. No live DB.
close_reason: Edge cases covered: malformed tnsnames (new tests/fixtures/tns/malformed + tns.rs malformed_tnsnames_recovers_without_panic + cli setup_discover_over_malformed_tnsnames_writes_read_only_config — recovers valid alias, no panic, writes valid READ_ONLY config via config-ops strict re-parse); duplicate/TCPS-wallet/IFILE/include-cycle/no-tnsnames-minimal-starter already covered by slices 1-3 (tns.rs + cli); permission-denied by search.rs unreadable_dir_is_skipped_not_fatal. Every produced config parses (config-ops revalidates) and stays READ_ONLY.

title: Unit tests: parse adapter, search, synth, secret-ref
description:
TEST. Comprehensive offline unit coverage for the discovery building blocks, driven by the CANONICAL synthetic fixture tree (design spec bead .1 item F, owned by the parse-adapter bead .3). Use the real TnsnamesReader, no parser mocks.

SCOPE.
- Search resolver (search bead): precedence, de-dup by canonical path, missing and unreadable candidates, empty-env fallback.
- Parse adapter (parse bead): service_names count, hint extraction (host, port, service, protocol, wallet), IFILE follow, include-cycle error, missing-file empty result.
- Profile synthesis (synth bead): deterministic naming, collision suffixing, alias-vs-EZConnect connect_string choice, default_profile selection for one vs many, strict-loader parse of the result.
- Secret-ref (secret-ref bead): per-profile env var name derivation and uniqueness, no literal: token anywhere, wallet_password_ref placeholder for TCPS.

ACCEPTANCE. All the above assertions pass against the shared canonical fixture (multiple aliases, one TCPS with wallet, one IFILE include, one duplicate, one EZConnect-style). Every generated profile is READ_ONLY. No secret material in outputs.

TEST PLAN. Standard cargo tests in the owning crates. Fixture is the shared tests/fixtures/tns/ tree from bead .3; do not create a divergent copy. No live DB, no mocks of the parser.
close_reason: Comprehensive offline unit coverage already delivered with the modules in slices 1-3, all against the shared canonical fixture, real TnsnamesReader (no mocks): search.rs (precedence, duplicates_collapse_to_highest_precedence, missing_candidate_is_reported_with_exists_false, unreadable_dir_is_skipped_not_fatal, empty_environment_never_panics); tns.rs (primary_fixture_enumerates_expected_services, tcps_descriptor_hints_incl_wallet, ez_connect_hints, duplicate_alias_takes_last_definition, ifile_included_alias_is_followed, include_cycle_returns_structured_error_not_panic, missing_file_is_empty_result_with_note); synth.rs (single_service_selects_default_profile, many_services_leaves_default_profile_unset_with_note, colliding_aliases_get_deterministic_suffixes, connect_string_is_alias/ezconnect, tcps_ezconnect_carries_scheme); secret-ref (required_env_vars_matches_profiles_including_wallet, oci_wallet_password_ref_only_for_tcps_or_wallet). Every generated profile READ_ONLY; no literal: token.

title: End-to-end onboarding test
description:
TEST. Prove the full path on a clean machine using the canonical synthetic TNS_ADMIN fixture (design spec bead .1 item F) and the config-ops backend, real components no mocks.

FLOW.
1. Create a temp TNS_ADMIN dir seeded from the canonical fixture tree (two net-services, one TCPS-with-wallet), point TNS_ADMIN and a temp ORACLEMCP_CONFIG target at it.
2. Run oraclemcp setup --discover --yes --json (exercises the agent/non-TTY JSON path, bead .13).
3. Assert: the target config was written THROUGH config-ops (a timestamped backup exists when a prior file was present; write is atomic), the JSON report lists both net-services with chosen profile names and env vars, both profiles are READ_ONLY env-ref with max_level and default_level SET to READ_ONLY, and the config parses via the strict loader.
4. Run oraclemcp doctor (offline): exit 0, no blocker, per-profile credential hints present.
5. Re-run setup --discover --yes: idempotent, no change, no clobber (merge bead .11).
6. Pre-seed an edited profile then re-run: edit preserved, only new profiles added.

REAL-SERVICE NOTE. Steps that need a live DB (doctor --online) run only when a real Oracle target and credentials are provided by the environment; otherwise the test stays offline and asserts the credential-missing hint. No mock database; use the real config-ops and real TnsnamesReader per testing-real-service-e2e discipline. The live leg follows the env-gated real-Oracle-XE pattern in crates/oraclemcp/tests/live_xe_service_attach.rs.

ACCEPTANCE. The scripted flow passes deterministically offline; the optional online leg passes when a real target is supplied. See crates/oraclemcp/tests/clean_machine_e2e.rs and e2e_harness.rs for structure.
close_reason: New crates/oraclemcp/tests/discover_e2e.rs: real binary + real config-ops + real TnsnamesReader over the canonical fixture, offline (live leg env-gated by ORACLEMCP_E2E_ONLINE). Proves the clean-machine flow deterministically: (1) setup --discover --yes --json writes READ_ONLY profiles via config-ops (write_mode fresh, both net-services + env vars, parses via OracleMcpConfig::from_toml_str, no literal:); (2) doctor offline reports action-needed + guides profile selection, doctor --profile validates + surfaces the connectivity/credential next-action; (3) re-run is idempotent (add_only_merge, zero profiles_created, profile count unchanged); (4) a hand-edited profile survives a re-run. Passes.

title: Consent-gate tests (no scan or write without consent)
description:
TEST. Lock the consent invariant: nothing is scanned or written without explicit consent, and the refusal is actionable.

CHECKS.
- Non-TTY without --yes or --discover-tns: setup --discover refuses with exit code 2 (usage_config_or_safety_block, per robot_docs cli_exit_codes_json) and a structured error naming the flag to pass; assert ZERO filesystem writes and that no tnsnames.ora candidate was opened for scanning.
- Non-TTY with --yes: proceeds and writes through config-ops.
- TTY answering No (piped simulated): nothing scanned or written.
- TTY answering Yes: proceeds.
- The prompt and the refusal both name the exact scan directories and the exact target path (operator_config_target_path at crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:2198).
- No secret value is ever read or printed on any consent path.

ACCEPTANCE. All the above hold; the refusal path leaves the filesystem untouched; the flag hint is present and correct; the refusal exit code is exactly 2.

TEST PLAN. CLI tests with pseudo-TTY vs piped stdin (crates/oraclemcp/tests/cli.rs harness). Assert side-effect-free refusal.
close_reason: Consent invariant locked: non-TTY without --yes/--discover-tns refuses exit 2 (setup_discover_non_tty_without_consent_refuses_human/_json — exact spec §D sentence, names the flag, no config written), refusal is side-effect-free before scanning even with a populated TNS_ADMIN (new setup_discover_refuses_before_scanning_even_with_tns_admin_present), explicit consent writes via config-ops, dry-run writes nothing, no secret value on any consent path. Refusal message is the pinned §D sentence naming the actionable consent flag.

title: TNS search-path resolver (candidate config dirs)
description:
TASK. Implement a pure-Rust resolver that enumerates candidate directories that may contain a tnsnames.ora, in the precedence set by the design spec, returning a de-duplicated ordered list with a per-candidate note (exists, unreadable, skipped).

BACKGROUND. Oracle Net looks for tnsnames.ora under TNS_ADMIN, then ORACLE_HOME/network/admin, then platform defaults. Today the doctor only reads TNS_ADMIN (crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:4752) and the wrapper script defaults ORACLE_NET_HOME to ~/.config/oraclemcp/network (crates/oraclemcp/src/robot_docs.rs:26). Discovery must look wider.

DESIGN and RATIONALE. Candidates in order: TNS_ADMIN, ORACLE_HOME/network/admin, ~/.config/oraclemcp/network, ~/ , /etc, common Instant Client locations, and the current working directory. Canonicalize and de-duplicate so a symlinked or repeated dir is scanned once. A permission error or missing dir yields a note, not an error, so discovery degrades gracefully. Pure fs plus std::env only, no driver calls, so it is trivially unit-testable and cross-platform (guard platform-specific default dirs behind cfg).

ACCEPTANCE. Given env overrides pointing at a temp dir, the resolver returns that dir first; duplicates collapse; a non-existent candidate is present in the report with an exists=false note; an unreadable dir is noted skipped and does not abort. No panics on missing HOME or ORACLE_HOME. Honors AGENTS.md pure-Rust and forbid-unsafe.

TEST PLAN. Unit tests with tempdir fixtures and scoped env: precedence order, de-dup by canonical path, missing and unreadable candidates, and empty-environment fallback. No live DB needed.
close_reason: Pure-std TNS search-path resolver in oraclemcp-config::discovery::search: candidate dirs (TNS_ADMIN, ORACLE_HOME/network/admin, ~/.config/oraclemcp/network, ~, /etc, Instant Client, cwd) in precedence order via injectable DiscoveryEnv; canonical-path de-dup (highest-precedence wins); per-candidate exists/missing/skipped status + note; permission-denied = skip-with-note (never fatal); no panic on missing HOME/ORACLE_HOME; platform dirs cfg-guarded. 6 unit tests green (precedence, de-dup, missing, unreadable, empty-env). forbid-unsafe.

title: Docs: README, example.toml note, configuration.md, behavior-inventory
description:
TASK. Document the onboarding and discovery flow for humans and agents.

SCOPE.
- README: a short Get started in minutes section describing oraclemcp setup --discover, the consent model, the env-var-per-service credential rule, idempotency and backup, and the zero-found fallback to the minimal starter.
- oraclemcp.example.toml: a header note pointing at setup --discover as the fast path and noting that discovery emits this same annotated shape (keep the file the canonical field reference; do not duplicate field docs).
- docs/configuration.md: a subsection on discovery: search order, net-service to profile mapping, connect_string alias-vs-EZConnect choice, secret-ref env naming, READ_ONLY defaults, and the merge and backup semantics.
- docs/behavior-inventory.md: add the observable behaviors of setup --discover (consent refusal shape, JSON report keys, backup artifact, idempotent re-run) so the behavior inventory stays complete.

RATIONALE. The feature is only adopted if it is discoverable and its safety posture is legible; the docs must state consent, no-secrets-on-disk, READ_ONLY defaults, and idempotency plainly.

ACCEPTANCE. Each of the four artifacts updated and internally consistent with the shipped flags and output; the example.toml note does not duplicate field docs; behavior-inventory lists the new observable behaviors. No secret examples use literal:.

TEST PLAN. Doc review checklist; if a docs link-check or example-parse test exists it stays green (example_config_parses.rs must still pass since example.toml only gains a comment note).
close_reason: Docs updated + internally consistent with shipped flags/output: README 'Get started in minutes' zero-config section (consent, env-var-per-service, idempotency+backup, zero-found fallback, --json/--dry-run, doctor follow-up); oraclemcp.example.toml header note pointing at setup --discover as the fast path (comment only — example_config_parses stays green, no field-doc duplication); docs/configuration.md new 'Zero-config TNS discovery' section (search order, mapping, connect_string alias-vs-EZConnect, env secret-ref naming, READ_ONLY defaults, merge+backup); docs/behavior-inventory.md new CLI-surface row (consent refusal exit 2, JSON report keys, backup artifact, idempotent add-only merge, READ_ONLY, env-refs). honesty_grep PASS; no literal: in examples; dashboard_e2e scanners green.

title: TNS parse adapter reusing TnsnamesReader
description:
TASK. Add a thin adapter that parses a tnsnames.ora using the upstream oracledb::protocol::net::connectstring::tnsnames::TnsnamesReader and returns, per net-service, a struct of name plus raw descriptor plus best-effort extracted hints (host, port, service_name, protocol, wallet-location hint for TCPS).

BACKGROUND. The parser to REUSE is at oracledb-protocol-0.5.1 src/net/connectstring/tnsnames.rs:15. It is re-exported as oracledb::protocol (oracledb-0.5.1 src/lib.rs:184). API: TnsnamesReader::read(config_dir) then service_names() and get(alias) and file_name(). It already handles IFILE includes, include cycles, comma-separated multi-line alias lists, upper-casing, and last-definition-wins. Do NOT hand-roll a TNS parser.

DESIGN DECISION and RATIONALE. The driver seam lint (scripts/oraclemcp_driver_seam_lint.sh DRIVER_PATTERN line 38; seam documented at crates/oraclemcp-db/src/connection.rs:9) forbids the oracledb:: path outside connection.rs. Two viable placements: (1) add a new module crates/oraclemcp-db/src/tns.rs using oracledb::protocol and add that file to BOTH seam allowlists (the shell ADAPTER_ALLOWLIST at seam-lint line 32 and the in-crate driver_seam test allowlist) with an inline justification; or (2) depend on the pinned oracledb-protocol crate directly (path oracledb_protocol:: does not trip the oracledb:: pattern because the underscore breaks it). RECOMMENDATION: option 1, keeping ONE driver crate and a single Oracle-facing boundary, since oraclemcp-db already depends on oracledb and on oraclemcp-config types. The implementer picks one and records the choice; either keeps the seam mechanically enforced. The descriptor-hint extraction (host, port, service, protocol) is best-effort parsing of the returned descriptor string used only for the human report and for choosing env-var names; the profile connect_string still stores the alias or a normalized EZConnect per the synth bead.

CANONICAL FIXTURE OWNERSHIP. This bead CREATES the shared canonical fixture tree defined in the design spec (bead .1 item F) under a committed tests/fixtures/tns/ path co-located with the adapter tests: a primary tnsnames.ora (multiple aliases, one TCPS descriptor with wallet_location, one EZConnect-style, one duplicate alias, one IFILE include of a second committed file) plus the include-cycle fixture. The edge-case (.16), unit (.17), and e2e (.18) beads REUSE this same tree, adding malformed and permission-denied variants as siblings; no bead creates a divergent copy.

ACCEPTANCE. Given the fixture dir, the adapter returns one entry per net-service with the raw descriptor and any extracted hints; IFILE includes are followed; an include cycle returns a structured error, not a panic; a missing file yields an empty result plus a note. The driver seam lint and the in-crate seam test both still pass. Honors forbid-unsafe.

TEST PLAN. Unit tests against the canonical fixture: assert service_names count, hint extraction (host/port/service/protocol/wallet), IFILE follow, cycle error, missing-file empty result. Add a seam-lint smoke assertion. No live DB.
close_reason: TNS parse adapter (oraclemcp-db::tns): reuses upstream oracledb_protocol TnsnamesReader (direct protocol-crate dep, oracledb_protocol:: path — driver-seam confined to connection.rs, lint+in-crate seam test both pass). Per net-service returns alias + raw descriptor (redacted Debug) + best-effort hints (host/port/service/protocol/wallet). Missing file = empty+note; IFILE cycle = structured TnsParseError (no panic). Canonical fixture tree tests/fixtures/tns/ created (primary TCPS+wallet/EZConnect/duplicate-last-wins/IFILE + cycle). 9 unit tests + seam smoke green; db api baseline refreshed (additive).

title: Consent model: TTY prompt, explicit flag, never scan-without-consent
description:
TASK. Implement the consent gate that governs whether discovery may scan the filesystem and whether it may WRITE a config. Scanning and writing without consent is forbidden.

BACKGROUND and RATIONALE. Discovery reads user files (tnsnames.ora across HOME and /etc) and writes a profiles.toml, both privacy- and safety-sensitive, so it must be explicitly authorized. Humans get an interactive TTY prompt; agents, CI, and any non-TTY caller must pass an explicit flag, because a silent auto-scan on a non-interactive invocation is a footgun. This mirrors the installers verify-before-mutate stance (maybe_run_doctor at install.sh:604 gates on interactivity and a yes flag).

DESIGN. Add flags on the setup or init command: --discover-tns to opt into scanning, --yes to grant non-interactive consent (both scan and write), and reuse the existing --json for machine callers. Consent resolution: if a TTY and no flags, prompt with the exact wording from the design spec, listing what will be scanned and where the config will be written, defaulting to No. If non-TTY and neither --yes nor --discover-tns, REFUSE with a clear structured error and a hint telling the caller which flag to pass; exit code 2 (usage_config_or_safety_block, per robot_docs cli_exit_codes_json). Consent to scan and consent to write are distinct steps: a user may scan and review a dry-run, then separately confirm the write.

ACCEPTANCE. TTY with no answer or No: nothing is scanned or written. TTY Yes: proceeds. Non-TTY without a flag: refuses with code 2 and a machine-readable error naming the flag; nothing scanned or written. Non-TTY with --yes: proceeds. The prompt text names the exact directories to be scanned and the exact target path (operator_config_target_path at main.rs:2198). No secrets are read or printed during consent. Honors the AGENTS.md fail-closed posture.

TEST PLAN. CLI tests driving a pseudo-TTY vs a piped stdin: refusal path emits the flag hint, exits 2, and writes nothing; --yes path proceeds; assert zero filesystem writes on refusal. See crates/oraclemcp/tests/cli.rs for the harness style.
close_reason: Implemented consent gate: scan/write consent distinct; non-TTY without --discover-tns/--yes refuses exit 2 with exact spec D wording; TTY interactive prompt logic; unit + CLI tests green

title: Net-service to profile synthesis
description:
TASK. Given the discovered net-services (from the parse adapter) and options, synthesize one oraclemcp ConnectionProfile per net-service with safe defaults.

BACKGROUND. ConnectionProfile fields at crates/oraclemcp-config/src/profile.rs:457. Accessor defaults: max_level and default_level default READ_ONLY (max_level at profile.rs:591, default_level at profile.rs:597); protected defaults false (profile.rs:603); mcp_exposed defaults true (profile.rs:625). connect_string may be an EZConnect string or a tnsnames alias (profile.rs:466).

DESIGN and RATIONALE.
- NAMING: derive a stable, lowercased, TOML-safe profile name from the net-service alias (for example net-service SALES_RO becomes profile sales_ro). On a name collision after normalization, suffix a deterministic disambiguator. Keep a deterministic mapping so re-runs are stable (needed by the idempotency bead).
- connect_string: store the tnsnames ALIAS as connect_string when the config will keep TNS_ADMIN reachable (the thin driver resolves aliases when TNS_ADMIN is set), OR store a normalized EZConnect host:port/service when the descriptor was cleanly parseable. The spec bead fixes the choice; default is alias plus a note that the wrapper sets TNS_ADMIN (setup_wrapper_template at crates/oraclemcp/src/robot_docs.rs:22). Record the decision per profile in the report.
- username: leave as a placeholder comment unless an obvious least-privilege convention applies; do not guess a real account.
- credential_ref: an env: placeholder (see the secret-ref bead); never literal.
- READ_ONLY safe defaults: SET max_level and default_level to READ_ONLY explicitly (per the read-only-safe-defaults bead .7), leave protected unset/commented (default false so the user can later opt a target up deliberately), leave mcp_exposed unset/commented (default true).
- default_profile: if exactly one net-service, select it; if many, leave default_profile unset (valid: the loader errors on an unknown default_profile per crates/oraclemcp-config/src/lib.rs:555) and note that the user should pick one.

ACCEPTANCE. Deterministic profile names; one profile per unique net-service; every profile is READ_ONLY (max_level and default_level SET to READ_ONLY), non-protected, env-ref; default_profile chosen only when unambiguous; output profiles validate through the loader. No secret material present. Honors the AGENTS.md safety invariant.

TEST PLAN. Unit tests: single vs many net-services, name normalization and collision suffixing, alias-vs-EZConnect connect_string choice, default_profile selection, and that a synthesized set parses via OracleMcpConfig::from_toml_str. No live DB.
close_reason: Implemented net-service→ConnectionProfile synthesis in oraclemcp-config/src/discovery/synth.rs: deterministic lower-snake name sanitization + collision suffixing, alias-vs-EZConnect connect_string choice (SynthOptions.tns_admin_reachable), env: credential_ref placeholder (never literal), explicit READ_ONLY max_level/default_level, default_profile chosen only when unambiguous. Config-owned DiscoveredNetService input keeps oraclemcp-config free of the oraclemcp-db dependency. 10 unit tests green; parses via OracleMcpConfig::from_toml_str.

title: Secret-ref placeholders and per-service env var guidance
description:
TASK. Make every generated profile carry an EXTERNAL secret reference and tell the user exactly which environment variable to set, per net-service.

BACKGROUND. Secret refs resolve through crates/oraclemcp-auth/src/secrets.rs (env: file: keyring: vault: literal:). resolve_secret_with at secrets.rs:173; literal: is rejected on protected profiles (secrets.rs:179; test literal_is_denied_under_protected_profile at secrets.rs:369). The minimal starter uses env:{credential_env} (setup_profiles_template at crates/oraclemcp/src/robot_docs.rs:17). For multiple discovered targets a single shared env var is wrong.

DESIGN and RATIONALE. Generate a distinct, deterministic env var name per profile, for example ORACLE_SALES_RO_PASSWORD for profile sales_ro, and set credential_ref to env:ORACLE_SALES_RO_PASSWORD. NEVER emit literal:. For a TCPS or wallet descriptor, also emit a commented wallet_password_ref env placeholder under [profiles.oci] (OciConfig.wallet_password_ref at crates/oraclemcp-config/src/profile.rs:91). The discovery report and the human and JSON output list, per profile, the exact env var to export before going live, plus the doctor and doctor --online commands to verify. Because the value lives only in the environment, no secret is ever written to disk, satisfying the no-secrets-on-disk requirement and the redaction posture of config-ops status.

ACCEPTANCE. Each profile has an env: credential_ref with a unique deterministic var name; no literal: anywhere; the report enumerates the env vars to set keyed by profile; TCPS descriptors additionally surface a commented wallet_password_ref env placeholder. Setting the named env var lets doctor --online resolve the credential. Honors the protected-profile literal ban even though generated profiles are non-protected.

TEST PLAN. Unit tests: env var name derivation and uniqueness, absence of any literal: token in output, wallet_password_ref placeholder emitted for a TCPS descriptor, and that the report env-var list matches the profiles.
close_reason: Extended synth.rs for secret-ref placeholders + per-service env-var guidance: DiscoveredNetService::is_tcps_or_wallet, DiscoveredProfilePlan.wallet_password_env_var populated as ORACLE_<NAME>_WALLET_PASSWORD for TCPS/wallet descriptors, and DiscoverySynthesis::required_env_vars() enumerating (profile,var) pairs. Every credential_ref stays env: (never literal). 5 new tests: deterministic unique env-var derivation, collision distinct vars, no literal token anywhere, wallet placeholder for TCPS/wallet-only (not plain TCP), env list matches profiles.

title: Read-only-safe defaults and fail-closed for unverifiable profiles
description:
TASK. Enforce that discovered profiles are READ_ONLY-safe and that a profile which cannot be verified stays READ_ONLY (fails closed), with least-privilege account guidance surfaced to the user.

BACKGROUND. AGENTS.md safety invariant: READ_ONLY is the default and the cap for unconfigured or protected profiles; protected profiles are pinned READ_ONLY with an immutable ceiling; the classifier gates every statement. profile.rs accessor defaults already make READ_ONLY the floor (max_level at profile.rs:591, default_level at profile.rs:597).

DESIGN and RATIONALE. Discovery must never emit a profile with max_level above READ_ONLY. The writer SETS max_level=READ_ONLY and default_level=READ_ONLY EXPLICITLY (not left to the accessor default) so the ceiling is legible in the file and cannot be loosened by a future default change; this is the safety posture stated visibly. Discovery does NOT set protected=true (it leaves protected commented/unset so the operator can later deliberately opt a specific target up to READ_WRITE), and it emits a commented protected=true hint teaching how to pin the ceiling immutably. The annotated writer emits least-privilege database-account guidance as a comment: create or use a dedicated read-only Oracle account with only CONNECT and SELECT, never an application-owner or SYSDBA account. It also emits the escalation-safety note that [audit] key_ref must be configured before raising any max_level above READ_ONLY (startup fails closed otherwise). When a profile cannot be verified (no credential env set, or the descriptor could not be confirmed), it is still written READ_ONLY and the report marks it needs-verification rather than dropping it or loosening it. This is the fail-closed rule applied to onboarding: unknown maps to least privilege, never to more.

ACCEPTANCE. No generated profile has max_level or default_level above READ_ONLY; both are SET to READ_ONLY explicitly in the rendered file. Least-privilege account guidance and the audit-before-escalation note appear as comments. An unverifiable profile is present, READ_ONLY, and flagged needs-verification in the report. Nothing generated contradicts the AGENTS.md safety invariant.

TEST PLAN. Unit tests: assert every synthesized and written profile is READ_ONLY on both levels and that the levels are explicitly SET (present in the rendered TOML, not merely defaulted), that the least-privilege comment and the audit-escalation note are present, and that an unverifiable input still yields a READ_ONLY profile flagged in the report.
close_reason: Read-only-safe defaults + fail-closed for unverifiable profiles. Synth now flags every profile needs_verification=true (discovery makes no live connection). Created discovery/writer.rs seed: read_only_safety_comment() carries least-privilege account guidance (dedicated CONNECT+SELECT account, never owner/SYSDBA) and the audit-before-escalation note ([audit].key_ref before raising max_level above READ_ONLY, or startup fails closed); render_annotated_config renders the bootable minimum with max_level/default_level SET explicitly to READ_ONLY plus per-profile needs-verification markers. 7 new tests: needs_verification universal, unverifiable bare alias stays READ_ONLY+flagged+present, levels SET in rendered TOML, guidance+audit note present, marker rendered, parses through strict loader, honesty framing clean. Writer full 25-field menu lands in .8.

title: Annotated safe-config writer (all fields, optional commented)
description:
TASK. Implement the writer that renders the full annotated profiles.toml: required fields present and set, every optional field present but COMMENTED with a one-line help string, self-documenting and bootable. It sits between the minimal starter (setup_profiles_template at crates/oraclemcp/src/robot_docs.rs:7) and the kitchen-sink oraclemcp.example.toml.

BACKGROUND. oraclemcp.example.toml is the canonical fully annotated reference; every field there has an inline one-line help comment and it is kept honest by crates/oraclemcp-config/tests/example_config_parses.rs. config-ops writes draft bytes VERBATIM (write_atomic_path at crates/oraclemcp-core/src/config_ops.rs:680) and re-parses with the strict loader (from_toml_str at config_ops.rs:280), so comments survive and an annotated file is fully compatible as long as it parses. The top-level OracleMcpConfig is deny_unknown_fields (crates/oraclemcp-config/src/lib.rs:83), so any UNCOMMENTED key MUST be a real serde field.

DESIGN and RATIONALE. Follow the field-disposition table in the design spec (bead .1 item C). Concretely:
- TOP LEVEL: SET schema_version=2 and default_profile (when unambiguous). Render monitor_profile as a commented one-line help (reconciled with oraclemcp.example.toml:35). Do NOT reproduce the full [http] and [audit] surfaces (that duplicates example.toml and the docs bead forbids duplication); emit a short commented pointer to oraclemcp.example.toml plus the safety note that [audit] key_ref must be set before any profile max_level is raised above READ_ONLY (startup fails closed without a key).
- PER PROFILE, cover ALL 25 ConnectionProfile serde fields (profile.rs:457 to profile.rs:550): SET name, description, connect_string, credential_ref (env: placeholder), max_level=READ_ONLY, default_level=READ_ONLY. SET username only when a least-privilege convention is known, else comment it. Render every remaining field COMMENTED with a one-line help string, each using its EXACT serde name so uncommenting yields a valid key: login_script, login_statements, trusted_session_statements, call_timeout_seconds, connect_timeout_seconds, sdu, protected, require_signed_tools, read_only_standby, mcp_exposed, dashboard_ddl_workbench, [profiles.session_identity], [profiles.pool], [profiles.oci] (with wallet_password_ref when a TCPS/wallet descriptor was seen, per the secret-ref bead), [profiles.drcp], [profiles.proxy_auth], [[profiles.app_context]], base.
Help strings are reconciled in MEANING with oraclemcp.example.toml (example.toml may SET some of these; the discovery writer comments them — reconciliation is on wording, not disposition). Reason for commenting optionals rather than setting them: a bootable minimum plus a self-documenting menu the user or agent can uncomment, without the writer guessing values it cannot know. The output MUST parse through OracleMcpConfig::from_toml_str.

ACCEPTANCE. The rendered config parses via the strict loader; serve starts and doctor passes offline against it; max_level and default_level are SET to READ_ONLY; every other ConnectionProfile serde field appears as a commented, help-annotated line using its exact serde name; monitor_profile appears commented; the [http]/[audit] pointer and the audit-escalation safety note are present; no unknown top-level keys; no secret literals. Round-trips through config-ops unchanged (comments preserved).

TEST PLAN. Covered by the parity/anti-rot test bead (.9) plus a golden snapshot of the rendered output for a two-net-service fixture. Assert strict-loader parse, that the commented-plus-set field set equals the ConnectionProfile serde field set, and that no unknown top-level key is emitted.
close_reason: Extended discovery/writer.rs render_annotated_config to the full annotated menu (design spec §C), driven by the disposition tables so it stays in sync with the structs. Top level: SET schema_version=2 + default_profile (when unambiguous, else commented); monitor_profile commented; http/audit example.toml pointer comments carrying the audit key_ref-before-escalation safety note. Per profile: all 25 ConnectionProfile serde fields — SET name/description/connect_string/credential_ref/max_level=READ_ONLY/default_level=READ_ONLY; username commented; every other field commented with a one-line help using its exact serde name (scalars before sub-table headers so uncommenting is valid); [profiles.oci] carries wallet_password_ref only for TCPS/wallet targets. Output parses via from_toml_str + honors deny_unknown_fields. Committed golden snapshot tests/golden/discovery_annotated.toml. 7 new tests + golden. Workspace test green except the pre-existing pwsh installer test.

title: Annotated-writer parity and anti-rot test
description:
