Session DELETE, principal revocation, or shutdown can drain the current map while a request is still in factory preparation; that request may then insert and execute an orphan lane after lifecycle closure returned. The creation marker does not yet carry tombstones or cancellation. Impact: cancelled sessions can perform late DB work and leak a capacity/generation lease until shutdown. Proposed fix: unify creation and closure in a per-key lifecycle protocol; lifecycle operations tombstone/cancel in-progress creation, builder completion revalidates before insert, and all permits/prepared generations drop outside registry locks. Guard tests: barrier DELETE/revocation/shutdown during builder yields no execution, lane, or retained capacity; a later valid reinitialize can create exactly one fresh lane.
close_reason: Confirmed and fixed with a generation-token lifecycle protocol. Every resolution holds strong session-key, principal, and global generation tokens; DELETE, principal revocation, and shutdown invalidate the scoped generation while draining the registry under one Lifecycle-to-Registry lock order. Candidate spawn stays outside locks, then final generation validation and insertion are linearized; invalid candidates, stale lanes, prepared generations, and capacity permits release outside locks. Weak token entries self-prune and valid same-key reinitialization receives one fresh generation. Added deterministic barriers proving DELETE, principal close, and shutdown cannot install/execute in-flight candidates, plus exactly-one fresh same-key reinit. Green: focused check/tests, workspace fmt/diff, workspace Clippy, deny, and complete workspace test suite (448 core plus all crates/integrations/trybuild/docs).

title: [QA100][P1][cancellation] Wake queued lane replies when caller cancels
description: Finding: recv_lane_reply polls the reply oneshot and then observes caller cancellation only through a synchronous Cx checkpoint. It never registers a cancellation waker, so a caller cancelled while queued can remain parked until some unrelated reply wake; in the adverse interleaving the lane dequeues and enters the dispatcher, and the caller/test can deadlock waiting for work that should have been cancelled. Broad workspace evidence: caller_cancellation_while_queued_prevents_dispatcher_entry stalled beyond 70 seconds; isolated reruns passed, consistent with a lost-wake race. Deterministic proof design: poll once with unsignalled reply and a counting task Waker, cancel the Cx, assert the task is woken, then poll to Cancelled. Proposed fix: poll a never-initialized asupersync OnceCell::wait(cx) cancellation sentinel alongside the reply, with reply-first ordering so authoritative terminal truth still wins. Guard tests: cancellation wakes a parked receiver exactly once; second poll returns Cancelled; simultaneous ready reply still wins; queued command never enters dispatch.
close_reason: Confirmed lost-wake race: a queued caller could park without registering on Cx cancellation, and the lane could dequeue before the caller republished cancellation. Fixed reply waiting with a public OnceCell cancellation sentinel and reply-first terminal ordering; retained a source-Cx clone solely as a read-only queued cancellation witness while keeping all database/factory effects on the lane-owned Cx. Deterministic tests prove external cancellation wakes the parked receiver and a source-cancelled queued command never enters dispatch; cancellation family and 30-run stress passed. Full pinned fmt/clippy/workspace-test/deny gate passed.

title: [QA100][P1][guard] Admit valid PACKAGE BODY and TYPE BODY create/replace units
description: Fresh verification while falsifying QA101 found a distinct create/replace defect. Normal valid stored-unit bodies are inconsistently rejected by the default guard: a trivial PACKAGE BODY with an anonymous BEGIN may pass, but a legal named terminator END p or a body containing subprogram declarations fails as MultiStatement/Unbalanced; valid TYPE BODY is likewise refused. oracle_create_or_replace advertises and detects PACKAGE BODY and TYPE BODY, while oracle_patch_source already has a bespoke body-shape override, so this is an availability/contract bug rather than the safety policy. Proposed fix: centralize token-aware stored-unit shape analysis for PACKAGE BODY/TYPE BODY, preserve DDL flooring and every dynamic-side-effect/trailing-SQL fail-closed check, and remove path-specific shape divergence. Guard tests: representative package/type bodies with member procedures/functions and named END classify Destructive/DDL; preview mints a DDL confirmation and performs zero I/O; confirmed apply executes exactly once. Negative tests keep malformed nesting, extra END, trailing DROP/GRANT, EXECUTE IMMEDIATE/file/network/scheduler markers, and anonymous-block controls forbidden. Verify editionable/noneditionable, AUTHID, comments, schema-qualified/quoted names, and patch-source/create-or-replace parity.
close_reason: Verified valid package/type bodies and foreign call specifications were rejected by divergent shape handling. Centralized token-aware stored-unit analysis, removed the patch-only override, preserved DDL/dynamic/trailing-SQL fail-closed gates, and added preview/apply/refusal/parity coverage. Full pinned workspace gates pass.

title: [QA100][P1][query] Stream bounded pages before Vec row materialization
description: Fresh QA89 completion audit found that the new encoded row budget drops an oversized aggregate Value, but ordinary positional and named read paths still fetch max_rows+1 OracleRow values into a Vec before page accounting. One individual cell Value is also materialized before its encoded size is known; max_col_width can be unset. Thus emitted row payloads are bounded, but driver and per-cell transient memory can still exceed max_result_bytes substantially. Proposed fix: extend the owned row stream or a visitor interface to positional and named ordinary reads, serialize/account each row before fetching the next, stop at row and byte boundaries without skip/repeat, and add backend-aware bounded cell encoding so text/LOB/nested values cannot allocate beyond the remaining page budget. Preserve flashback teardown, cancellation quarantine, named binds, complex-value fallback, and byte-identical normal pages. Guard tests: first-row and later-row oversize, 5000 wide rows, one unbounded text cell, nested cursors/LOBs, exact boundary, named and positional binds, cancellation/backpressure, driver fallback, and a peak-memory assertion proving O(page byte cap plus one bounded cell), not O(max_rows times cell caps).
close_reason: Confirmed Vec materialization in ordinary positional/named reads. Added production one-row driver paging with immediate byte accounting, bounded expandable-cell materialization, cursor-safe resume, named-bind parity, fallback compatibility, and unit/live guard tests.

title: [QA100][P1][query/http] Enforce a whole MCP response byte budget
description: Fresh QA89 completion audit established that max_result_bytes is enforceable in oraclemcp-db only as the sum of compact row-object payloads. Columns, array separators, pagination metadata, sealed cursors, structured tool results, JSON-RPC or SSE framing, and replay-store cloning are added later and remain outside that cap. The registry is now truthful about this narrower law, but the original whole-response availability goal remains. Coordinate with open QA100 bead .52, which specifically bounds stateful replay retention. Proposed fix: define one core dispatch response-budget type with a documented minimum structural allowance; charge DB payload, metadata, cursor, tool envelope, negotiated MCP or SSE framing, and retained/replayed representation with checked arithmetic. Oversized results become a bounded typed error or resource reference before replay insertion; never advertise a cap smaller than the unavoidable error envelope. Guard tests cover exact/cap+1 pages, wide metadata, sealed cursors, JSON and SSE, stateful replay, stateless and stdio, nested data, concurrency/global accounting, and measured serialized bytes at the actual wire boundary.
close_reason: Enforce a whole-MCP-response byte budget. New crate::response_budget module: ResponseByteBudget charges the ACTUAL serialized wire bytes (serde_json::to_vec) of the finalized JSON-RPC response — envelope (payload embedded twice: fenced content[0].text + structuredContent), column metadata, cursor, JSON-RPC frame + reserved transport framing — before any transport writes it or the replay store retains it. In-budget -> passes through byte-identical; oversized (or non-serializable, or overflow) -> fail-closed to a bounded typed -32001 'response too large' error whose own size is proven <= OVERSIZED_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES (giant request id dropped to null; ceiling clamped so it can never be below the error envelope it must emit; compile-time assert). Wired at every response boundary: stdio dispatch (server.rs:1222/1279) and both HTTP outcomes Ok+Err (http/mod.rs:6400/6402). Default ceiling 64 MiB sits above the legit ~50 MiB max page (25 MiB row cap embedded twice). 10 module unit tests + 168 lines of http tests. Green on main: core lib 570/0, full core suite 0 failed (golden HTTP unchanged), fmt+clippy clean.

title: [QA100][P1][plugin] Bound plugin helper descendant pipes and process trees
description:
Finding

The plugin runner repeats QA9: it waits only for the direct child and then unconditionally joins stdin/stdout/stderr workers. A plugin that exits successfully after spawning a descendant with inherited stdout or stderr can therefore exceed the advertised timeout indefinitely; timeout/error cleanup kills only the direct child and can leak descendants/readers.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/plugin.rs:166-245 uses wait_timeout for the direct child followed by unconditional writer/stdout/stderr joins.
- QA9 fresh static fourth-instance audit found the identical successful-parent/pipe-inheriting-descendant control flow after fixing iam_token.
- Existing tests cover direct never-exit and large response/stdin deadlock, not successful parent plus inherited descendant pipe.

Impact

A configured plugin can wedge or exhaust the MCP process beyond its deadline and accumulate descendants/workers across calls.

Proposed fix

Reuse the reviewed command-group process group/Windows Job Object containment from QA9. Apply one end-to-end deadline to direct wait and every I/O worker, kill and reap the whole tree on every success/timeout/error exit, cap output, and preserve redacted typed errors.

Guard tests

- Successful parent with stdout-inheriting and stderr-inheriting descendants returns/fails within deadline and leaves no descendant.
- Repeated direct timeouts leave no process or reader growth.
- Existing finite, oversized, malformed, crash, and stdin-before-output cases remain green.
- POSIX and Windows cfg paths compile and use equivalent whole-tree ownership.
close_reason: Confirmed by fresh runtime reproduction: a 250ms invocation took 2.007s when a successful parent left either stdout or stderr inherited by a descendant. Fixed with command-group POSIX process-group/Windows Job containment, one wait-and-I/O deadline, whole-tree termination/direct-child reap on every exit, capped drains, and redacted errors. Added 10x inherited-pipe stress, 10 repeated timeout-tree leak checks, output-cap/redaction guards, and preserved all existing plugin tests; focused 14/14 tests and plugin-clean clippy passed.

title: [QA100][P2][admission] Add pre-authenticated remote control ingress
description: Residual after QA95: the shared listener can safely reserve pre-auth ingress for the default local operator and local doctor/readiness traffic because peer loopback is server-derived. It cannot guarantee reserved availability for an allow-listed remote OAuth operator: authority is learned only after request parsing, while hostile remote sockets can arrive before authentication. QA95 deliberately refuses remote pre-auth reserve rather than trusting a path/header/subject claim. Add a separately bounded control ingress whose privilege is established before general request parsing (for example a dedicated control listener with mandatory mTLS and allow-listed certificate identity, or an equivalent OS-authenticated channel). Keep the ordinary listener fail-closed. Guard tests must saturate ordinary workers with hostile remote peers, prove an allow-listed remote operator and readiness channel still enter, prove unauthenticated peers cannot occupy the control reserve, enforce absolute handshake/header deadlines, and prove total workers never exceed configured caps.
close_reason: Fresh-eyes confirmed QA95 deliberately leaves remote operator reserve unavailable. Added optional [http.control]/--control-listen as a second mandatory-mTLS listener with registered-and-operator-allowlisted certificate identity established before HTTP parsing; independent bounded pre-auth handshake and authenticated operator/doctor ledgers; absolute TLS/header/body deadlines; control-only route classification; shared graceful shutdown. Guards saturate hostile ordinary workers and control handshakes, prove remote operator/readiness entry, reject no-cert/unregistered/non-operator/MCP traffic, and lock caps/deadline release. Green: focused 2/2, core lib 617/617, config 117 + parity 5 + example, binary 120/120, strict core+binary all-target clippy.

title: [QA100][P2][secrets] Bound keyring helper time and output
description:
Finding

OS-keyring secret resolution invokes both the operator-selected ORACLEMCP_KEYRING_COMMAND and platform keyring helpers with std::process::Command::output, imposing neither a wall-clock deadline nor stdout/stderr size caps. A wedged helper can block startup or reconnect forever; a noisy helper can allocate unbounded memory.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-auth/src/secrets.rs:225-265 selects the environment override or platform helper and funnels both through run_keyring_command.
- run_keyring_command calls Command::new(...).args(...).output() synchronously. output waits for process exit and buffers complete stdout and stderr into memory.
- Linux secret-tool and macOS security use that same path at lines 268-281.
- main resolves these references for database credentials and several security/operator secrets at main.rs:805, 1209, 1501, 2401, and 3914; database request timeouts do not bound a resolver that runs before connection.
- Keyring tests exercise parsing and an injected resolver seam, but no hanging, oversized-output, child-cleanup, or redaction behavior of the production command runner.
- A full unlimited Beads search found the original SecretResolver feature and onboarding references, but no keyring command deadline/output defect.

Impact

A locked GUI keyring, broken secret service, buggy platform helper, or misconfigured operator override can indefinitely wedge service startup, reconnect, configuration validation, or security-key initialization. Unbounded helper output can exhaust server memory. These failures occur on a trusted local integration seam, but they defeat service liveness and can be triggered repeatedly by normal recovery paths.

False-positive checks

Direct argv execution avoids shell injection and the helper is operator-configured; neither property bounds a legitimate executable that hangs, prompts, forks, or writes excessive data. The IAM token helper already documents and partially implements the need for deadlines and caps, confirming the expected local-command safety posture.

Proposed fix

Create one reusable bounded direct-command runner for secret and token helpers: closed stdin, one end-to-end wall deadline, independently capped stdout and stderr, process-group or platform-job cleanup, direct-child reap, descendant-safe output collection, and redacted typed errors. Keep argv direct and never include secret bytes in logs or errors.

Guard tests

1. A helper that sleeps or waits for stdin returns a typed timeout within a bounded margin.
2. stdout and stderr larger than their caps fail cleanly without excessive allocation.
3. A helper that exits after spawning a pipe-inheriting descendant cannot wedge joins and leaves no process/thread leak.
4. Normal one-line secret, empty output, invalid UTF-8, and nonzero exit have documented results.
5. Sentinel secret/output bytes never appear in Debug, Display, logs, or protocol errors.
6. Platform helper and ORACLEMCP_KEYRING_COMMAND both use the same hardened runner.
close_reason: Confirmed by fresh production-runner reproduction: direct sleep and successful parents with stdout- or stderr-inheriting descendants each took about 2.006-2.007s without any deadline. Hardened the single keyring runner used by the environment override and Linux/macOS adapters with closed stdin, direct argv, a five-second end-to-end deadline, POSIX process-group/Windows Job containment, whole-tree termination/direct-child reap on every exit, independently capped output, zeroized stdout, discarded stderr, typed failures, and redacted errors. Added hang, 10x inherited-pipe, cap, UTF-8/nonzero, redaction, stdin, and 10-retry process/worker-leak guards. Full auth 56/56, doctests, rustfmt, and all-target clippy -D warnings pass.

title: [QA100][P1][SIEM] Require confidential transport for audit shipping
description:
Finding

Audit SIEM shipping accepts an arbitrary plaintext http:// endpoint and sends both the configured secret authentication header and signed audit payload to it. No configuration or runtime boundary requires TLS for a remote host.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-config/src/lib.rs:173-227 documents HTTP(S), stores the endpoint as an unchecked String, and validates only that a destination exists and auth options accompany an endpoint.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:1490-1516 resolves siem_auth_header_ref to a secret and attaches its value verbatim to the forwarder for any endpoint scheme.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/audit_shipping.rs:84-181 clones the endpoint and headers into the outbound POST without a transport-security check. The body contains the signed audit record and, by design, may include SQL preview and subject/decision metadata.
- The only network test explicitly proves a plaintext loopback HTTP URL is accepted; there are no remote-HTTP rejection or secret-plus-HTTP tests.
- Documentation recommends HTTPS in examples but repeatedly calls the field HTTP(S), so this is accepted behavior rather than a parser accident.
- A full unlimited Beads search found no SIEM TLS or cleartext-transport task.

Impact

A plausible configuration typo or internal HTTP endpoint transmits the SIEM ingest token and privileged audit metadata in cleartext to network observers and intermediaries. Possession of the token may permit forged ingest or access to the SIEM endpoint; exposed audit previews and identities can reveal schema and operational activity. Protected profiles do not change this transport behavior.

False-positive checks

Shipping is operator-configured and off by default, but safe-by-default handling of secret material should reject an insecure remote destination rather than rely on a comment. Plaintext loopback can be useful for tests or a same-host proxy; it can be permitted under an explicit narrow rule without allowing remote cleartext. Signed records provide integrity, not confidentiality.

Proposed fix

Parse and validate the endpoint once. Require HTTPS for every non-loopback host and whenever a secret auth header is present. Permit loopback HTTP only under a documented local policy; if a broader insecure override is truly required, make it explicit, noisy, default-off, and forbidden for protected profiles. Do not follow redirects to a less secure scheme and validate the effective destination used by the HTTP client.

Guard tests

1. Remote http:// with and without siem_auth_header_ref is rejected by default.
2. https:// endpoints are accepted; malformed and unsupported schemes fail with actionable errors.
3. Loopback HTTP behavior is explicit and covered, including auth-header policy.
4. HTTPS-to-HTTP redirect or endpoint rewriting cannot downgrade the request.
5. Protected profiles cannot enable an insecure override.
6. Errors and logs include only the safe endpoint/reason and never the auth value or payload.
close_reason: Confirmed: remote plaintext and redirect downgrade could expose signed audit records and SIEM auth. Added validated HTTPS/literal-loopback policy, HTTPS-only auth headers, no-redirect runtime behavior, redacted diagnostics, docs, and regression tests.

title: [QA100][P1][audit] Move SIEM network delivery off the audit mutex
description:
Finding

Durable audit append holds the global chain-state mutex while the shipping decorator performs synchronous SIEM network POSTs. An unreachable or slow endpoint therefore adds up to the per-record network timeout to privileged actions and head-of-line blocks all other auditing, despite documentation saying shipping is off the request hot path and failures are nonfatal.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/sink.rs:604-650 locks Auditor state before sink.append and retains that lock across sink.flush for durable records. The lock is necessary for sequence/hash serialization, but its scope includes the entire decorated flush.
- crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/shipping.rs:221-240 fsyncs local storage and then synchronously calls forward_pending before returning.
- forward_pending at lines 197-219 loops over every pending record and calls each forwarder serially.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/audit_shipping.rs:99-181 builds a runtime and blocks on one HTTP POST with a five-second default timeout. Its comment at lines 136-139 incorrectly calls this off the request hot path.
- Production privileged paths call Auditor::append with durable=true synchronously, including dispatch/mod.rs:2661 and core query_execute.rs:248/271; they cannot execute until append returns.
- Existing shipping tests cover order and non-propagation of failures, not latency isolation, slow readers, concurrency, queue bounds, or restart delivery.
- A full unlimited Beads search found the original shipping feature and reactor fix but no network-under-audit-lock issue.

Impact

A slow, unreachable, or maliciously slow-reading SIEM can add roughly five seconds per durable audited action and serialize unrelated lanes behind the auditor mutex. A pending batch can multiply that delay. Because shipping errors are swallowed only after waiting, an optional fail-safe mirror becomes a service-wide availability dependency and can cause request pileups.

False-positive checks

Local fsync must remain synchronous and fail closed before privileged execution; moving that step would weaken the invariant. Remote shipping is explicitly a nonfatal mirror, so its network completion is not required before database execution. Forwarding outside only the shipping pending mutex is insufficient because the outer Auditor mutex remains held.

Proposed fix

Keep local append/fsync and chain-head advancement synchronous. After local durability, enqueue an immutable signed record to a bounded durable spool and let a dedicated supervised worker deliver in order with retry/backoff. Release the Auditor mutex before network I/O. Define overflow, shutdown, restart replay, observability, and destination-reconfiguration semantics; never silently discard without a durable local indicator.

Guard tests

1. A slow/unreachable forwarder does not materially delay local durable append or an audited action.
2. Concurrent audited lanes preserve a gap-free local chain while shipping is stalled.
3. Queue/spool is bounded, exposes depth/failures, and applies the documented overflow policy.
4. Restart replays undelivered records in sequence without duplicate local audit entries.
5. Multiple forwarders preserve per-destination order and isolate one slow destination.
6. Local append/fsync failure still fails closed before execute, proving the core safety invariant remains intact.
close_reason: Confirmed Auditor held its chain mutex through synchronous SIEM/WORM delivery. Fixed with per-destination bounded durable file spools and supervised ordered workers; local audit append/fsync remains synchronous fail-closed, external I/O is asynchronous and nonfatal. Added durable overflow indicators, exclusive spool ownership, restart recovery/order, destination binding, WORM replay idempotence, SIEM idempotency headers, observability, and shutdown persistence. Guards cover slow/unreachable isolation, concurrent gap-free local chain, bounded overflow, restart replay, multi-destination isolation, reconfiguration/ownership, worker panic retry, WORM tail dedupe, and local flush failure. Audit 123/123, core SIEM 7/7, focused spool 9/9, clippy/check/deny green.

title: [QA100][P2][audit] Harden audit files against symlinks and permissive modes
description:
Finding

The audit log, lock sidecar, and anchor temporary file are opened with ordinary path-following APIs and without enforced private file modes. In a shared or attacker-writable configured audit directory, preplanted symlinks can redirect writes or truncate an operator-writable target; under permissive umask/custom layouts, security records can also be created world-readable.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/sink.rs:100-141 opens <audit>.lock with create/read/write following symlinks, then after acquiring the fd lock calls set_len(0) and writes the PID. A symlink target is therefore truncated and replaced with PID text.
- FileAuditSink::open at sink.rs:230-248 follows an audit-path symlink and creates the log without an explicit Unix mode or a post-open ownership/type check.
- crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/anchor.rs:138-164 opens the fixed <audit>.anchor.tmp path with create(true), truncate(true), and no no-follow/create-new protection before rename. A preplanted symlink can be repeatedly truncated on durable appends.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:1324-1338 uses create_dir_all for the audit parent and then opens the sink. Unlike FileStore, it does not enforce 0700 directories or 0600 files.
- The default audit path is computed under XDG state, but build_auditor runs before FileStore::open_default hardens the state root; read-only configurations with an explicit audit key may never open FileStore. Custom /var/log or WORM-style layouts remain entirely outside FileStore hardening.
- A full unlimited Beads search found prior audit locking/resume work but no symlink/no-follow or audit-mode issue.

Impact

A local user able to write the configured audit directory can cause startup or each durable anchor update to clobber another file writable by the oraclemcp account. An audit-path symlink can divert or corrupt the security record. Permissive modes can expose SQL previews, subject identifiers, outcomes, and operational metadata to other local users. Default private layout mitigates much of the steady-state exposure but does not make custom/shared paths safe.

False-positive checks

The attacker needs local directory-write access or an unsafe operator-selected layout; this is not a remote vulnerability. Advisory flock prevents two cooperating writers but does not prevent path redirection. Atomic rename protects the final anchor replacement, not the initial truncate of a symlinked fixed temporary path. Documentation tells operators to protect the log, but the safe-by-default sink should fail closed on unsafe filesystem objects.

Proposed fix

Centralize secure audit-path opening. Validate parent ownership/type/mode; reject symlink and non-regular targets with symlink_metadata plus post-open identity checks; use no-follow/openat-style APIs where supported. Create lock/data/temp files with 0600 and create_new, use unpredictable same-directory temp names for anchor replacement, fsync directories, and validate link count/owner according to the platform policy. Harden acceptable existing modes or fail with an actionable diagnostic.

Guard tests

1. Preplanted symlinks for the audit file, .lock, and .anchor.tmp cause fail-closed startup/update and leave target bytes unchanged.
2. FIFO, directory, device, and unexpected hard-link cases are rejected without blocking.
3. New audit, lock, anchor, and temporary files are 0600 and default directories are 0700 on Unix under umask 000 and 022.
4. Existing overly broad modes are hardened or rejected according to the documented policy.
5. Normal restart, lock contention, anchor update, and audit verify remain crash-durable and cross-platform.
6. Custom shared/WORM paths get an explicit ownership/mode diagnostic rather than silently weakening safety.
close_reason: Harden audit files vs symlinks + permissive modes, mirroring file_store.rs. sink.rs AuditLogLock::acquire + FileAuditSink::open used plain OpenOptions (followed symlinks, no mode); anchor.rs used a fixed <anchor>.tmp with create+truncate (preplanted symlink truncated on every durable update); main.rs build_auditor used plain create_dir_all. Added reject_unsafe_existing (symlink_metadata pre-check), harden_open_regular_file (post-open fstat identity + fchmod 0600, cfg(unix)), private openers (O_CREAT|O_EXCL, append, lock), unpredictable hidden same-dir anchor tmp created exclusively, and create_private_audit_dir (reject symlink/non-dir, 0700 create, harden existing broad dir). Tighten-only. 4 guard tests: symlinked log/lock fail closed + target intact, dir/fifo rejected, 0600-create + harden-existing. Green: oraclemcp-audit 142/0, oraclemcp lib 246/0 + cli 27/0, fmt + clippy (audit + oraclemcp) clean. Worktree-agent co-authored.

title: [QA100][P1][HTTP] Mark dashboard and MCP cookies Secure on TLS
description:
Finding

Both privileged dashboard-session cookies and stateful MCP-session cookies omit the Secure attribute even when the server is using native HTTPS. Cookies are host-scoped rather than port-scoped, so a later plaintext HTTP request to the same hostname can disclose them to another service or a network observer.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/dashboard_auth.rs:431-434 always renders oraclemcp_dashboard_session with Path=/, Max-Age, HttpOnly, and SameSite=Strict, but no Secure.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:1575-1577 does the same for the stateful MCP cookie at Path=/mcp.
- The dashboard cookie is emitted by the pairing/login path at http/mod.rs:1875; the MCP cookie is emitted from sse_response at lines 5946-5961.
- Native TLS requests run through serve_https_until at lines 6101 onward but reuse the same HttpTransportConfig/response functions; no secure-transport bit reaches either cookie formatter.
- SameSite=Strict limits cross-site requests and HttpOnly blocks script reads, but neither prevents a browser from sending the cookie over plaintext HTTP to the same host. Browser cookies are not isolated by port.
- Existing tests assert HttpOnly/SameSite behavior but never assert Secure for HTTPS or define trusted reverse-proxy scheme handling.
- Independent HTTP review confirmed the path, and a full unlimited Beads search found no Secure-cookie task.

Impact

The dashboard cookie is a long-lived authenticated operator session and its session view yields CSRF/action tickets for guarded dashboard routes. If it is exposed through same-host plaintext navigation, subresources, a second local service, or network interception, an attacker can impersonate the operator within the remaining session policy. An exposed MCP session identifier can disclose or interfere with stateful transport state when combined with the applicable principal context.

False-positive checks

Loopback-only plaintext HTTP is an intentional supported mode, so unconditionally adding Secure would break local http:// browser use. The defect is that native HTTPS is not distinguished and therefore gets the weaker cookie too. Reverse-proxy termination cannot safely trust arbitrary X-Forwarded-Proto; it needs an explicit trusted-proxy/external-scheme contract.

Proposed fix

Carry an authenticated transport-security flag into cookie generation and automatically append Secure for native TLS. Add an explicit fail-closed external HTTPS/trusted-proxy setting for terminated TLS rather than trusting caller headers. Consider __Host- cookie names for dashboard sessions, which require Secure, Path=/, and no Domain. Document the constrained loopback plaintext exception and avoid setting privileged cookies remotely when the effective scheme is insecure.

Guard tests

1. Native HTTPS dashboard pairing and MCP initialize responses include Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite=Strict.
2. Loopback plaintext behavior is explicit and remains usable only within the intended policy.
3. Remote plaintext mode cannot mint privileged cookies.
4. Proxy mode ignores spoofed forwarding headers unless the peer/config is trusted and emits Secure only for a validated external HTTPS scheme.
5. Cookie names/path/domain satisfy __Host- rules if adopted, and logout/expiry uses matching attributes.
6. Browser-level regression verifies cookies minted on HTTPS are not sent to same-host HTTP.
close_reason: Confirmed TLS cookies omitted Secure. Added server-derived effective scheme, native rustls forcing, explicit trusted termination assertion with no forwarded-header trust, Secure dashboard/MCP cookies, loopback-only plaintext compatibility, remote plaintext suppression, matching DELETE expiry, docs and native/request regressions. Full workspace gates green with repo-local TMPDIR.

title: [QA100][P1][HTTP] Bound stateful session registries and reject failed initialize allocation
description:
Finding

Stateful HTTP initialization creates entries in two unbounded in-memory maps, with no global or per-principal session cap. The insertion predicate is only that the parsed request method string equals initialize, so even a JSON-RPC initialize that the server rejects receives and stores a live session.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:901-981 defines HttpSessionStore as an unbounded HashMap; its idle reap scans the full map and collects/removes expired keys.
- HttpResultStore at lines 1002-1129 has a second unbounded session HashMap. Only events within one session are capped at 128.
- handle_mcp_post_exchange extracts method before dispatch and pre-mints a session id for every initialize at lines 5188-5229.
- After dispatch, every non-busy JSON-RPC response goes to sse_response. At lines 5919-5961 that function checks only method == initialize, not whether response.result is a successful initialize result, then inserts into both maps and sets a cookie/header.
- Repeated successful initializes also allocate indefinitely. They do not open database lanes, so the per-principal lane cap does not apply. The request limiter defaults to a 1200 burst and 600/s, while the idle TTL defaults to 900 seconds; sustained authorized traffic can retain roughly 540,000 empty sessions before expiry.
- Both HTTP and HTTPS listener loops invoke the O(n) idle scan about once per second.
- Existing tests cover once-per-session reinitialize, per-session event bounds, deletion, and idle expiry, but no failed-initialize allocation or aggregate session admission.
- Two independent reviewers confirmed the path; a full unlimited Beads scan found only closed worker/lane/session-binding features, not a pre-lane session-registry cap.

Impact

A valid but abusive client, or an unauthenticated client in an explicitly open deployment, can consume large amounts of memory and force repeated full-map CPU scans without using Oracle connections. As the registries grow, their mutex-protected operations and one-second reaper can degrade all stateful clients and eventually terminate the process through memory pressure.

False-positive checks

The 900-second TTL bounds retention time but not cardinality within the window. The 64 accepted-worker cap limits simultaneous connections, not sequential initialize requests. The 600/s rate limiter controls velocity but still permits a very high retained population and performs no aggregate admission. Per-session event capping does not cap empty sessions.

Proposed fix

Mint and insert a session only after validating a successful initialize response. Add coordinated global and per-principal session admission limits before allocation, returning the standard 429/AtCapacity envelope with retry guidance. Treat session and result-store entries as one lifecycle transaction so partial insertion cannot leak. Use an expiry structure whose steady-state reap cost is proportional to expirations rather than total population, and preserve operator/health reserve.

Guard tests

1. Parse errors, invalid jsonrpc, invalid initialize params, and other rejected initialize responses create no id, cookie, owner, or result-store entry.
2. The N+1 successful initialize for one principal and globally is rejected before allocation with stable retry metadata.
3. DELETE, credential revocation/rotation, idle TTL, and shutdown remove both store entries in lockstep and release capacity.
4. A request storm cannot exceed the configured map cardinality or cause unbounded reap work.
5. Caps are keyed only by server-derived bounded-cardinality principal identity and cannot starve operator/doctor reserve.
6. Valid initialization and protocol-version negotiation remain MCP-conformant.
close_reason: Confirmed rejected and repeated initialize requests allocated unbounded state. Session ids are now minted only after a successful negotiated initialize result; coordinated global/per-principal admission creates session and replay entries atomically; an expiry index makes reap proportional to expirations; DELETE, principal rotation/revocation, idle expiry, shutdown, and late completions release both registries without resurrection. Failure/cap/storm/lifecycle guards pass, full core 517/517 and strict all-target clippy pass.

title: [QA100][P1][recovery] Make FileStore service locks crash-recoverable
description:
Finding

The shared FileStore .service.lock is a create-new sentinel removed only by Rust Drop. SIGKILL, power loss, abort, or a failed construction after file creation leaves it behind permanently, and every future state mutation reports Locked until an operator manually deletes the file. Its unconditional Drop removal can also unlink a replacement lock acquired after manual stale cleanup.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/file_store.rs:204-221 acquires by create_new, writes PID/owner text, and never takes an OS advisory lock or validates liveness. Any existing path maps directly to FileStoreError::Locked.
- ServiceLock::drop at lines 105-110 unconditionally removes the pathname; it does not prove that the path still refers to the file/owner originally acquired.
- If write, sync_all, or fsync_dir fails after create_new, acquire_service_lock returns an error and the local File is dropped, but no ServiceLock exists to remove the sentinel.
- Long-lived WriteIntentLog and ClientCredentialStore plus config/proposal/source-history/backup mutations all depend on this lock.
- The closed B3.3 stale-lock bead and service_lifecycle.rs tests cover the distinct service-instance.json guard. That implementation checks dead PIDs and has a regression test proving an old guard Drop cannot delete a replacement. FileStore has neither behavior.
- Existing FileStore tests cover only two simultaneous live owners and clean Drop/reopen. A full unlimited tracker search found no separate .service.lock crash-recovery issue; QA100 .1 is the distinct one-process composition bug.

Impact

A routine hard crash can prevent writable service startup, client-credential startup, config operations, proposals, source snapshots, and backup indefinitely. Manual recovery is undocumented and risky because a still-live owner is indistinguishable from a stale sentinel. If an operator clears/replaces the file while an old owner lives, the old Drop can delete the new owner token and permit a third writer, violating single-writer safety.

False-positive checks

Process cleanup closes file descriptors but does not unlink ordinary filesystem entries. The lock is not flock/LockFileEx-backed. The repo already treats the analogous service-instance crash case as a bug and implements stronger replacement-safe semantics there. This finding does not propose weakening live-owner exclusion.

Proposed fix

Prefer a cross-platform OS advisory lock held by an open descriptor, with metadata only as an operator hint; process death then releases ownership automatically. If sentinel takeover remains, bind it to an unpredictable owner token plus PID start identity, verify liveness without PID-reuse ambiguity, and remove only when path identity/token still matches. Clean up all partial-acquisition errors durably. Provide a safe doctor/status recovery path. Coordinate this with QA100 .1 so one process owns one shared service-lock capability.

Guard tests

1. A second live process cannot acquire the same root.
2. A child process acquires then is SIGKILLed/forcibly terminated; a fresh process immediately reacquires without manual deletion.
3. Failures while writing or fsyncing lock metadata leave no permanent sentinel.
4. Removing/replacing a stale lock while the old ServiceLock object exists, then dropping the old object, leaves the replacement intact.
5. PID reuse or malformed metadata never causes takeover of a provably live owner.
6. Backup/config/client-credential/write-intent integration recovers after simulated crash while preserving cross-process exclusion.
close_reason: Confirmed: replaced crash-stale create-new sentinel with descriptor-owned cross-platform advisory OS lock; added live-process exclusion, 10x forced-crash recovery, partial-initialization release, and replacement-identity regression guards.

title: [QA100][P1][dashboard] Close dedicated-tool bypass of browser DDL release gate
description:
Finding

The browser-specific DDL/Admin release gate examines only oracle_execute SQL. The same paired browser action routes explicitly allow oracle_compile_object, oracle_create_or_replace, and oracle_patch_source on confirm/execute, so those dedicated DDL tools bypass the promised pre-dispatch browser release gate.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:4822-4855 includes oracle_compile_object, oracle_create_or_replace, and oracle_patch_source in browser-reachable action allowlists for preview, confirm, and execute.
- dashboard_workbench_release_gate at lines 4690-4717 returns immediately for every tool other than oracle_execute. It therefore never classifies or blocks the dedicated source-mutation tools.
- handle_operator_action_route invokes that gate at lines 4576-4581 and then proceeds into idempotency and MCP dispatch. There is no later browser-origin check; the dedicated tools execute through their normal DDL ceiling/confirmation path.
- README.md:389-394 and docs/operations.md:785-787 state that browser-originated DDL/Admin apply is release-gated and rejected before MCP dispatch; they do not exempt structured DDL tools.
- The only release-gate test at http/tests.rs:2238-2307 sends raw CREATE TABLE through oracle_execute. No table-driven test covers the other guarded mutation tools.
- A paired browser can send an arbitrary allowlisted tool payload to /operator/v1/actions/confirm or /execute with its route-scoped ticket; UI visibility is not an authorization boundary.
- Independent HTTP review confirmed the path. A full unlimited tracker search found closed no-bypass acceptance work but no bug covering the dedicated-tool gap.

Impact

A paired dashboard session can apply PL/SQL/view compilation, full CREATE OR REPLACE source, or source patches in a release line that tells operators browser DDL apply is disabled. Profile ceiling, classifier, single-use confirmation, and audit still apply, so this is not an unrestricted SQL bypass; it is a material violation of the additional browser release gate and deployment policy.

False-positive checks

The tools are intentionally available to non-browser operator API callers and must remain so. Preview is intentionally allowed from the browser. The defect is confirm/execute authorization being keyed to one tool name rather than the danger/action class. Dedicated source tools are explicitly documented as DDL and can mutate Oracle when execute=true with a grant.

Proposed fix

Make the browser gate policy-driven and fail-closed across every operator action target. Classify raw SQL where present and attach authoritative mutation/required-level metadata to structured tools; reject every browser confirm/execute whose maximum effect is DDL/Admin unless the fully wired profile-level opt-in is enabled and all promised controls pass. Derive allowlists and release-gate coverage from one typed tool policy table so new tools cannot silently escape.

Guard tests

1. Paired-browser confirm and execute are rejected before dispatch for oracle_compile_object, oracle_create_or_replace, oracle_patch_source, and DDL/Admin oracle_execute when the opt-in is off.
2. Preview remains allowed and returns the normal classifier/grant information.
3. Non-browser authenticated operator callers retain normal ceiling/confirmation behavior.
4. If dashboard_ddl_workbench opt-in is implemented, tests require both HTTP and profile flags, reject protected/read-only profiles, and preserve audit/idempotency/confirmation.
5. A registry contract fails whenever a mutation-capable tool lacks an explicit browser preview/confirm/execute policy.
6. Existing DML/read browser behavior and core classifier invariants remain unchanged.
close_reason: Confirmed three structured DDL tools bypassed the browser-only release gate. Replaced route-specific string allowlists with one typed per-tool route and browser-apply policy table; browser confirm/execute now fail closed before idempotency or dispatch for compile/create-or-replace/patch and classifier-proven DDL/Admin SQL, while unclassifiable SQL also fails closed. Paired-browser table covers both routes and all four mutation targets; structured preview remains forced non-executing; non-browser structured DDL, DML, and reads remain unchanged; policy uniqueness/coverage is locked. Focused browser, preview, non-browser, Admin/DML/read/policy tests and strict core clippy pass; rustfmt/diff checks pass.

title: [QA100][P1][security] Reject empty and undersized HMAC keys
description:
Finding

Both security-critical HMAC consumers accept empty or trivially short resolved secrets. An empty OAuth HS256 key makes attacker-minted admin JWTs verifiable; an empty or guessable audit key removes the keyed anti-forgery property of the audit chain.

Evidence and reachable paths

- crates/oraclemcp-auth/src/secrets.rs:119-128 and 154-166 wrap empty env values as Secret; lines 210-214 accept an empty or newline-only file after line-ending removal.
- crates/oraclemcp-config/src/lib.rs:408-424 validates that the secret reference string exists, not the resolved key material.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:2385-2424 copies resolved bytes directly into Hs256Verifier.
- crates/oraclemcp-auth/src/oauth_rs.rs:70-80 and 229-253 compute valid HMAC-SHA256 for any key length, including zero.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:1199-1227 copies [audit].key_ref bytes directly into SigningKey. The legacy direct ORACLEMCP_AUDIT_KEY path rejects only zero length and still accepts one byte.
- crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/record.rs:310-337 exposes an infallible SigningKey::new with no strength invariant.

Impact

With http.oauth.hs256_secret_ref pointing to an empty environment value or zero-byte/newline-only file, the service starts and the effective HS256 key is public knowledge. An attacker can mint a token with the configured issuer, audience, and oracle:admin scope and bypass transport authorization up to the profile ceiling. A weak audit key also lets an actor who can alter the log recompute signatures, contradicting the documented protection against recompute-from-genesis forgery.

False-positive and duplicate checks

HMAC mathematically permits arbitrary key lengths, but these are authorization and tamper-evidence keys, not generic HMAC test vectors. README instructs operators to use a long random secret, confirming the intended posture. Two reviewers traced the resolved-byte path independently. Repo-local bead search found no empty/weak/key-length issue.

Proposed remediation

Introduce context-specific validated key constructors or startup validators for OAuth HS256 and audit signing. Require at least 32 bytes of secret material after resolution and line-ending normalization; do not impose that policy on unrelated database passwords. Make invalid key material fail startup with a structured, redacted and actionable error. Prefer private key fields/validated constructors so library callers cannot bypass the invariant; this project explicitly favors clean API migration over compatibility shims.

Guard tests

- Empty env value, empty file, and newline-only file all fail closed.
- 1-byte and 31-byte OAuth/audit keys are rejected; 32-byte and longer keys are accepted.
- Writable and protected startup returns the documented structured error without echoing the reference locator or secret.
- A known empty-key forged JWT is rejected because configuration cannot construct an enforcement verifier.
- Audit SigningKey construction cannot produce a forgeable empty-key signer.
- Existing valid OAuth issuer/audience/scope tests and audit rotation/verification tests remain green.
notes:
Additional validated consumer

The protected custom-tool signature key has the same invariant gap. crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:1133-1163 resolves ORACLEMCP_CUSTOM_TOOLS_HMAC_KEY and accepts zero- or one-byte material even for protected profiles; crates/oraclemcp-config/src/custom_tools.rs:379-480 signs/verifies with arbitrary bytes; the sign-tool CLI path at crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:3834-3865 also accepts empty input. Apply the same >=32-byte validated constructor/startup boundary here. Add load/startup and sign-tool tests proving 0, 1, and 31 bytes are rejected and 32 bytes is accepted without leaking the key.
close_reason: Verified reachable weak-key construction at OAuth, audit startup/verify, and protected custom-tool signing boundaries. Added opaque >=32-byte HMAC key type, fallible production constructors, redacted structured failures, 0/1/31 reject and 32/33 accept tests including newline-only files, migrated every caller, and documented the requirement. Full pinned workspace fmt/clippy/test/API/deny gates pass.

title: [QA100][P1][audit] Record operator API attempts and terminal outcomes truthfully
description:
Finding

Every authorized operator API request is durably recorded before routing with decision=Allowed and outcome=Succeeded. The eventual route result is never appended, so unknown routes, malformed bodies, release-gate refusals, missing lanes, downstream MCP errors, and internal failures are permanently represented as successful operator actions.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:1727-1785 authenticates/rate-limits, calls append_operator_audit, then dispatches handle_operator_api_route.
- append_operator_audit at lines 2138-2160 hard-codes AuditDecision::Allowed and AuditOutcome::Succeeded before any route-specific validation or side effect.
- crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/record.rs:18-39 defines Allowed as allowed and run, Pending as logged but not executed, Succeeded as executed successfully, and Failed as execution failed.
- Tests intentionally show GET /operator/v1/sessions returning 404 while adding an operator_api record, and the lane-cancel test sends an unknown lane that returns 404 after the pre-record. Neither asserts the outcome, so the hard-coded Succeeded survives.
- Browser DDL release-gate 403, invalid JSON 400, unsupported/action-tool rejection, config/store failures, and MCP tool isError responses all happen after the same successful pre-record.
- Dedicated guarded tools may append their own accurate mutation audit, but read/control routes and rejected edge attempts rely on this operator record; duplicate records do not repair the false one.
- Independent HTTP review confirmed the path. A full unlimited Beads search found no operator-outcome truth task.

Impact

Incident responders and SIEM rules cannot distinguish a successful operator mutation/control action from a rejected or failed attempt. Dashboards can claim a lane was cancelled, config was applied, or a route succeeded when nothing happened. This weakens the audit chain as an authoritative security record and can conceal attack/error patterns behind false Succeeded entries.

False-positive checks

Recording authorized attempts before dispatch is desirable and required for fail-closed control actions; the bug is labeling an attempt as terminal success. Unauthorized requests rejected before operator authority are intentionally not recorded as Allowed. HTTP status alone is insufficient for MCP responses whose transport is 200 but result.isError is true.

Proposed fix

Use a two-phase append-only audit protocol: durably append an operator Pending/attempt record before side effects, execute/route, then append a linked terminal Succeeded, Failed, Blocked, RolledBack, or uncertainty outcome derived from the authoritative route/MCP result. Include a correlation/request hash or parent sequence without raw secrets. Define what happens when the terminal append fails: mutation routes must preserve the existing fail-closed/uncertainty posture and surface an actionable response.

Guard tests

1. Successful read/control/mutation routes emit Pending plus the correct terminal outcome and correlation.
2. 400, 403 release-gate, 404 unknown route/lane, 409 store conflict, 429, 500, cancellation, panic, and MCP isError produce truthful non-success outcomes.
3. A crash/fault between pre-record and completion leaves an honest Pending record, never Succeeded.
4. Terminal audit failure around side effects follows documented fail-closed or uncertainty semantics.
5. SIEM CEF/syslog severity and dashboard audit-tail render the terminal outcome correctly without double-counting actions.
6. Core guarded-tool audit remains authoritative and can be correlated without conflicting success claims.
close_reason: Verified true and fixed end-to-end: operator API now durably appends hash-linked schema-v7 Pending and terminal records with server-generated request correlation + parent sequence; terminal classification covers HTTP, operator-domain, JSON-RPC/MCP isError, refusal, partial, cancellation and panic; authorized rate limits are recorded; terminal-audit failure after side effects returns explicit indeterminate state while retaining honest Pending evidence. CEF/syslog and audit-tail carry correlation, dashboard coalesces completed pairs without hiding unmatched Pending. Guards cover success, 400/403/404/409/429/503, MCP failure/refusal, partial apply, cancellation/panic, interrupted attempt, and fault-injected post-side-effect terminal append failure. Audit 113/113, core QA20/full gates, focused clippy, web 23/23, deny and formatting checks passed.

title: [QA100][P2][dashboard] Issue an action ticket for the shipped lane kill switch
description:
Finding

The dashboard ships a per-lane Kill control and a guarded POST /operator/v1/lanes/cancel route, but dashboard sessions never receive a route-scoped action ticket for that path. The client throws before sending any request, so the advertised kill switch is unusable from the UI.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/operator_protocol.rs:84-90 declares POST /operator/v1/lanes/cancel, and http/mod.rs routes it to the guarded cancellation handler.
- web/src/app/App.tsx:615-637 wires the Kill button to cancelLane and presents the success/error state.
- web/src/app/operator-client.ts:861-868 calls generic operatorPost for /operator/v1/lanes/cancel.
- operatorPost at lines 1338-1356 resolves the route ticket before fetch; actionTicketFor at 1360-1368 throws missing dashboard action ticket when absent.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/dashboard_auth.rs:48-63 is the only source used to mint DashboardSessionView.action_tickets, and it omits /operator/v1/lanes/cancel.
- Existing Rust cancellation tests use loopback operator authority directly, not a paired browser session. Dashboard source/e2e checks do not click the control.
- The closed lane-cancel feature bead required a backing route and UI control but did not capture the ticket-list integration. A full unlimited search found no follow-up duplicate.

Impact

Operators cannot terminate a stuck or dangerous lane from the Sessions dashboard. Clicking Kill always yields the client-side missing-ticket error and the server never sees the request, undermining a high-value incident-response control.

False-positive checks

Omitting the ticket is not an intentional security denial: the UI explicitly renders the action, the route is operator-authorized/audited, and the original feature bead says the UI control should be real. Adding a route-scoped ticket preserves CSRF and same-origin defenses rather than bypassing them.

Proposed fix

Add the lane-cancel route to the dashboard action-ticket policy. Better, derive dashboard-ticket eligibility from the authoritative typed OperatorRouteSpec metadata with an explicit browser_post flag, so server routes, generated client contracts, and tickets cannot drift. Keep default-deny for routes not marked browser-capable.

Guard tests

1. A paired DashboardSessionView includes exactly one POST ticket for /operator/v1/lanes/cancel.
2. Browser E2E clicks Kill, sends CSRF plus the route ticket, receives termination, and refreshes active lanes.
3. Missing/wrong/cross-route/replayed tickets remain rejected according to policy.
4. A contract test compares every operatorPost path reachable in the shipped SPA with the server browser-ticket allowlist.
5. Non-browser operator authorization and cancellation audit semantics remain unchanged.
close_reason: Confirmed the dashboard Kill control could never obtain a route ticket. Added explicit browser-post metadata to the authoritative operator route table, derived session tickets and default-deny validation from that table, enabled lane cancel, and locked exact route uniqueness plus every shipped operatorPost path. Rust route-scope/default-deny tests, direct web fetch header/body regression, TypeScript, generated contract, strict core clippy, and rustfmt checks pass.

title: [QA100][P2][dashboard] Restore capabilities and global source search actions
description:
Finding

The shipped dashboard client calls oracle_capabilities for the Sessions page and oracle_search_source for Explorer global search, but the server action-execute allowlist rejects both tool names before MCP dispatch. Both visible features therefore fail with operator_action_tool_not_allowed.

Evidence

- web/src/app/operator-client.ts:1079-1088 posts oracle_capabilities to /operator/v1/actions/execute; App.tsx:546-556 polls it for the selected lane every ten seconds.
- operator-client.ts:1157-1172 posts oracle_search_source; App.tsx:4107-4142 uses it for global source search.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:4822-4855 defines the only action-tool allowlist. EXECUTE includes related connection, schema, object, DDL, source, query, and static PL/SQL tools but omits both names.
- operator_action_target returns operator_action_tool_not_allowed before dispatch whenever the tool is absent.
- oracle_search_source is a registered read tool and oracle_capabilities is the server-answered discovery tool; README.md:952/955 documents both. docs/behavior-inventory.md:50-54 specifically claims the operator capability posture and Explorer global source search are aligned.
- Existing HTTP allowlist tests cover selected metadata tools but omit these two. Dashboard tests grep client/server source rather than exercising the calls end-to-end.
- Independent HTTP review confirmed the path. A full unlimited tracker search found no allowlist-drift follow-up.

Impact

Sessions cannot display live lane capability posture, and Explorer source search always fails even though the UI offers it. Repeated capability polling also creates avoidable error traffic and confusing degraded-state UI.

False-positive checks

Both are read/discovery operations, not intentionally MCP-only mutation tools. The shipped SPA explicitly calls them through the supported generic action route, and the behavior inventory calls the surfaces aligned. The server-level implementation of oracle_capabilities is special but still reachable through normal MCP tools/call once action authorization permits it.

Proposed fix

Add both tools to the typed browser execute policy with read-only metadata. Replace hand-maintained string arrays with a generated/validated operator-action policy derived from the authoritative tool registry plus explicit entries for server-answered tools. Default-deny remains correct for tools not deliberately exposed.

Guard tests

1. Paired-dashboard calls to fetchLaneCapabilities and fetchExplorerSourceSearch reach dispatch and return expected structured responses.
2. A contract test enumerates every tool string used by operator-client.ts and proves it has the intended preview/confirm/execute policy.
3. Registry additions do not become browser-exposed automatically without explicit policy review.
4. Read tools cannot enter confirm-only or mutation policy classes, and forbidden tools remain rejected.
5. Behavior-inventory alignment is generated or checked against this contract.
close_reason: Restore capabilities + global source search dashboard actions. OPERATOR_ACTION_TOOL_POLICIES lacked oracle_capabilities and oracle_search_source, so the dashboard's capabilities view and global source search failed closed (default-deny). Both are real registered read-only tools (registry.rs/robot_docs.rs); added them with routes=ACTION_EXECUTE_POLICY + BrowserApplyPolicy::Allow, matching the other read-only info/search tools (search_objects/get_source) — execute route only, never a mutating confirm. Regression test asserts both are allowed on execute, denied on confirm, and default-deny still holds for unlisted tools. NOTE: the bead also suggested replacing the hand-maintained table with a registry-derived/validated policy; deferred as a larger refactor (the authoritative tool registry lives in the oraclemcp binary crate, not oraclemcp-core, so core cannot import it without an architectural change) — filed as the remaining enhancement, the actual default-deny bug is fixed. Green: core policy tests 2/2, fmt + clippy clean.

title: [QA100][P2][idempotency] Never evict the retried or in-progress operator action
description:
Finding

At exactly the 1024-entry operator idempotency capacity, begin prunes an entry before checking whether the incoming key already exists. Retrying the oldest completed or in-progress key therefore evicts its own protection and is treated as Fresh, re-entering MCP dispatch instead of replaying or reporting in-progress. The eviction routine may also discard any in-progress action to admit unrelated new work.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:665-703 locks the ledger, calls prune_operator_idempotency_entries, and only afterward looks up facts.storage_key.
- prune at lines 782-795 removes the oldest entry while len >= 1024, without considering the incoming key or whether response is None (in progress).
- At len 1024, a retry of the oldest key causes that exact entry to be removed, lookup returns None, and a new Fresh lease is inserted.
- complete at lines 706-718 silently does nothing if its lease was evicted, so the original response may never become replayable.
- The operator action route dispatches every Fresh lease through tools/call. Downstream confirmation grants/durable intents provide additional protection for committing SQL, but the documented edge contract also covers control actions and response replay and must not rely on every downstream tool being idempotent.
- Existing tests cover normal replay/conflict/in-progress below capacity but no 1023/1024/1025 boundary or in-progress eviction.
- Independent HTTP review confirmed the ordering. A full unlimited Beads search found no capacity-eviction idempotency issue.

Impact

Under high dashboard/operator churn, retries can execute the action path again, lose the original response, or report inconsistent results. Evicting an in-progress entry specifically removes concurrent-duplicate suppression. Core single-use grants often prevent a second committing SQL execution, but this is defense by accident and does not cover every operator control/read side effect or future tool.

False-positive checks

A bounded cache legitimately evicts completed old entries after its advertised TTL/capacity policy. It must first honor an exact existing key, and it must never evict an in-progress entry merely to admit new work. The bug is the lookup/eviction order and state-blind eviction, not the 1024 bound itself. Durable write intents are explicitly documented as a separate crash-safety layer rather than a replacement for HTTP retry identity.

Proposed fix

Look up and resolve an existing key before any capacity eviction. For a genuinely new key, remove only expired or completed entries; never evict response=None entries. If capacity is occupied by live/in-progress work, reject the new action with 429/AtCapacity and retry guidance. Use deterministic LRU/expiry metadata and make completion detect impossible missing leases loudly.

Guard tests

1. At exactly 1024 entries, retrying the oldest completed key replays the original byte-equivalent response.
2. Retrying the oldest in-progress key returns operator_idempotency_in_progress and never dispatches again.
3. Filling capacity cannot evict any in-progress entry; a new key receives bounded-capacity rejection.
4. 1023/1024/1025 boundary tests cover completed eviction order, TTL expiry, conflict, and completion-after-churn.
5. Concurrent model/property test proves at most one Fresh lease exists per storage key.
6. Guarded SQL still retains single-use grant and durable-intent defenses independently.
notes:
Additional validated capacity dimension

The same OperatorIdempotencyLedger caches the complete HttpResponse for as many as 1024 entries for 15 minutes with no byte budget, and its facts retain caller-controlled request_id strings. Operator actions can wrap large MCP read responses, so item-count correctness alone can still retain many GiB; long but accepted body/header identifiers add avoidable amplification. Extend this bead remediation with per-entry/global resident-byte ceilings, a small validated idempotency/request-id length and charset, and honest non-cacheable behavior for oversized completed reads. Never solve the byte ceiling by evicting response=None/in-progress entries. Add large-response and long-ID boundary/concurrency tests.
close_reason: Never evict the retried/in-progress operator idempotency entry. OperatorIdempotencyLedger::begin pruned BEFORE the key lookup, and prune evicted oldest-by-created_at with no in-progress check — so under capacity/TTL pressure it could drop (a) the very entry being served (prune-before-lookup) or (b) an in-progress entry (response None), letting a retry double-execute. Split into prune_expired_operator_idempotency_entries (TTL-only retain, runs before lookup so an expired key reads as absent) and evict_completed_operator_idempotency_entries_to_capacity (runs only on the fresh-insert path AFTER the lookup; evicts only completed entries via filter(response.is_some()), never in-progress; cap may be briefly exceeded if all in-progress, which is separately bounded by concurrency limits). Unit test fills past cap with completed entries + one older in-progress and asserts the in-progress survives while completed are evicted. Existing idempotency replay/conflict/in-progress tests still green. Green: core idempotency 3/3, fmt + clippy clean.

title: [QA100][P2][HTTP] Bound operator event streams and validate lane identities
description:
Finding

The operator SSE replay store caps events within each stream but never caps, expires, or removes streams. An authorized caller controls lane_id and every GET /operator/v1/events inserts a new subject-hash plus lane-id map entry even when that lane does not exist, enabling unbounded retained memory.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:562-629 defines OperatorEventStore.streams as an unbounded HashMap keyed by subject hash and lane id. MAX_OPERATOR_EVENTS_PER_STREAM=128 bounds only each value vector.
- append_snapshot_and_resume uses streams.entry(key).or_default and appends a relatively rich health/metrics/classifier snapshot on every request. There is no last-used timestamp, stream-count limit, eviction, or lifecycle removal.
- operator_events_response at lines 4355-4410 invokes that insertion for every authorized events request.
- operator_event_lane_id at lines 4413-4428 accepts any caller-supplied nonempty, non-slash string up to 128 bytes and does not require an active or special known lane.
- Cursor isolation prevents cross-lane replay but does not constrain cardinality. Listener/subject request rate limits bound creation speed, not lifetime count.
- Existing tests cover the 128-event ring and cursor behavior, not stream churn, invalid/nonexistent lane admission, or removal.
- Independent HTTP review confirmed the path. A full unlimited Beads search found no operator stream-cardinality issue.

Impact

A valid but noisy operator credential or paired session can create arbitrary unique lane ids until memory is exhausted. Even normal lane churn leaks one map entry and up to 128 snapshots per subject/lane for the process lifetime. The global mutex and map growth also increase latency for legitimate event clients.

False-positive checks

The server intentionally keys rings by subject and lane for replay isolation, and retaining bounded recent events per live lane is sound. The defect is missing lifecycle/cardinality control and accepting nonexistent identifiers, not the per-stream ring design. The route is authenticated, so severity is scoped as an authenticated availability weakness.

Proposed fix

Validate requested lane_id against the server-owned active-lane registry or one explicit operator aggregate stream. Add global and per-subject stream caps, last-used TTL/LRU, and lifecycle deletion on lane close, credential revoke, logout, and shutdown. Refuse new streams at capacity with the standard AtCapacity envelope rather than evicting active subscribers silently.

Guard tests

1. Unknown/random lane ids are rejected without allocating a stream.
2. Churning more than the configured per-subject/global limit keeps map cardinality bounded and returns stable capacity errors.
3. Lane close, principal close/revoke, TTL expiry, and shutdown remove associated rings.
4. Existing cursor isolation, 128-event retention, stream-gap, and cross-lane rejection remain correct.
5. Active subscribers are not evicted by unrelated churn, and operator reserve remains available.
6. Metrics expose current stream count, evictions/expirations, and capacity rejections without raw subject ids.
close_reason: Bound operator event streams + validate lane identities. OperatorEventStore.streams was an unbounded HashMap keyed by (subject_hash, lane_id) — per-stream events were capped (128) but the NUMBER of distinct streams was not, and operator_event_lane_id accepted any well-formed non-empty lane_id, so a caller could mint unbounded distinct streams from attacker-chosen lane ids. Two fixes: (1) validate lane_id at the call site — a specific lane_id must name a currently active lane (checked against operator_active_lanes_data); only the default aggregate 'operator' stream is always valid; a bogus/inactive lane returns 404 operator_lane_not_active. (2) hard cap MAX_OPERATOR_EVENT_STREAMS=256 with least-recently-updated eviction (added last_updated to a new OperatorEventStream value struct) as defense-in-depth against closed-lane/many-operator accumulation. Tests: inactive-lane 404 + default/active 200; store caps stream count under MAX+50 distinct lanes; existing lane-scoped resume/gap tests updated to declare their lanes active (new StaticLaneLifecycle::with_lanes). Green: core lib 578/0, fmt + clippy clean.

title: [QA100][P2][restore] Make restore an exact transactional replacement
description:
Finding

Service restore is implemented as an in-place recursive merge after stopping the service, not the documented directory replacement. Files absent from the backup survive, and any mid-copy/start error returns immediately with a partially restored tree and the service left down; there is no staging, rollback, quarantine, or restart recovery.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp/src/service_lifecycle.rs:801-827 runs stop, restore_from_manifest, then start sequentially with ?. Any restore or start error exits the function after the service was stopped.
- restore_from_manifest at lines 1063-1100 mutates the live state tree first, then config, then external audit paths one by one.
- copy_dir_restore at lines 1103-1110 delegates to the snapshot copier, which creates/overwrites files present in the backup but never removes or moves files absent from it.
- Thus post-snapshot credentials, proposals, source snapshots, write-intent files, and unknown future state remain after restoring an older backup.
- docs/backup-restore.md:1-6 defines restore as a directory replace; lines 69-93 explicitly instruct moving the current state aside and retaining the pre-restore copy until verification. The CLI does neither.
- Existing tests mutate files that also exist in the backup; they do not seed an extra live-only file or fault-inject each copy/rename/start boundary.
- QA100 .4 covers untrusted manifest paths/integrity preflight. This finding remains after perfect preflight because runtime I/O/start failures and replacement semantics are distinct. A full unlimited Beads search found no transactional-restore follow-up.

Impact

An intended rollback can silently retain newer security-sensitive state and produce a hybrid system that never existed in either snapshot. Disk-full, permission, race, or service-start failures can strand a stopped service with a partially old/partially new state/config/audit combination and no automatic way back. This undermines disaster recovery exactly when operators need deterministic recovery.

False-positive checks

An additive merge can be an intentional migration strategy, but the documented and original bead contract says stop, restore dir, start and tells operators to move the old tree aside. No schema-aware merge policy is defined. Atomic replacement must preserve the no-delete rule operationally by quarantining the prior tree, not destroying it.

Proposed fix

After QA100 .4 preflight, stage the complete target state/config/audit into private same-filesystem locations, fsync and verify them, then stop the service only for a short commit phase. Atomically rename the current tree/config/audit to timestamped pre-restore quarantine and swap the staged exact replacement into place. On any commit/start failure, roll back swaps and best-effort restart the old service; retain both trees and emit actionable recovery metadata. Handle cross-filesystem paths as an explicit multi-resource transaction with a journal/recovery state.

Guard tests

1. A live-only post-backup file is absent from the restored active tree and present in pre-restore quarantine.
2. Fault injection at every create/write/fsync/rename/stop/start boundary yields either the complete old running state or complete restored running state, never a hybrid.
3. Failed start rolls back to old state and attempts old-service restart; failures preserve recovery artifacts and instructions.
4. Successful restore verifies exact manifest inventory, config, audit chain/anchor, permissions, and service health before declaring success.
5. Crash during commit is recoverable idempotently from a small durable transaction journal.
6. Dry-run proves all paths/filesystems/space/permissions without stopping or mutating the service.
close_reason: Make restore an exact transactional replacement. qa4 (false-friend) verified the manifest signature + staged SHA-256-verified tempfiles + refused post-backup extra files, but apply() still wrote staged files one-by-one directly into the live state dir then config/audit/anchor — a disk-full at file 50/100 left a hybrid tree with the service stopped, unrecoverable. Rewrote apply() as a two-phase transaction: Phase 1 stages the COMPLETE replacement tree into a private sibling .restore-staging.<pid>.<ns> dir (reusing write_prepared_file temp->fsync->rename->dir-fsync) + fsynced sibling temps for out-of-tree config/audit/anchor (cap-std O_EXCL nofollow), zero live mutation; Phase 2 quarantines the prior tree to .pre-restore.<pid>.<ns>, swaps the staged tree in with a single atomic rename, then quarantine-then-rename each out-of-tree file — every rename recorded (rename_tracked); any failure triggers rollback_restore (reverse renames) restoring the original tree whole (never hybrid), then run_restore best-effort restarts the service and surfaces the retained quarantine path. Post-commit verify_committed_state_tree re-checks dir (dev,ino) continuity + exact inventory. qa4's refuse-extra-files replaced by exact replacement (post-backup live-only files land in the retained quarantine). Tighten-only: symlinked state dir/targets refused. HONEST RESIDUAL: dir swap is std's unavoidable two-rename (no renameat2 RENAME_EXCHANGE — needs unsafe, banned); a process kill EXACTLY between the quarantine + swap renames is recoverable via the durably-named .pre-restore/.restore-staging siblings (manual, not an auto-replayed journal) — guard-test #5 (idempotent crash recovery from a durable journal) not fully automated; every other intent met. 2 new tests (exact-replacement+quarantine; mid-commit-failure full-rollback no-hybrid). Green: bin service_lifecycle 27/0, oraclemcp lib 246 + bin 119, fmt+clippy clean. Worktree-agent co-authored.

title: [QA100][P2][backup] Snapshot audit log and anchor as one consistent pair
description:
Finding

A hot service backup does not coordinate with the audit writer and can copy an in-state audit log before its anchor. If a durable append occurs between the two copies, the backup contains log through sequence N but an anchor attesting N+1, a state the audit contract classifies as truncation/tampering and that restore refuses.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp/src/service_lifecycle.rs:723-751 takes only FileStore .service.lock while recursively copying the state tree. The auditor uses a separate <audit>.lock advisory lock in oraclemcp-audit/src/sink.rs:87-141, so appends continue.
- copy_dir_snapshot_inner sorts directory entries by filename. In the usual state/audit directory, audit.jsonl sorts before audit.jsonl.anchor and is copied first.
- Auditor durable ordering is record fsync then anchor update. A record appended after the log copy but before the anchor copy yields an anchor ahead of the copied log.
- oraclemcp-audit/src/anchor.rs:275-306 explicitly treats chain length below anchor.seq as AnchorViolation::Truncated. Its module contract says anchor-ahead cannot be produced by the audit writer crash protocol, making the backup look tampered.
- For an audit path outside state, copy_audit_for_backup intentionally copies anchor before log, which yields the acceptable anchor-behind state under concurrent appends; the in-state recursive path lacks that discipline.
- docs/backup-restore.md:61-66 advertises hot backup as acceptable and self-consistent per file, but restore needs a consistent cross-file log/anchor pair.
