title: W1: add golden behavior harness
description: Freeze client-visible behavior before replacing transports or runtime. Add golden stdio transcripts for initialize, failed initialize with missing/wrong init token, notifications/initialized, tools/list, oracle_capabilities, unknown tools, successful tool results, and structured error envelopes. Add golden HTTP cases for /mcp initialize, protected-resource metadata, unauthorized WWW-Authenticate behavior, host/origin guards, JSON response mode, and Streamable HTTP session behavior. Normalize only protocol-approved variable fields and keep secrets out of fixtures.
design: Implementation shape: freeze current user-visible MCP behavior before any transport rewrite. Use current rmcp/axum implementation as the baseline, but do not encode accidental nondeterminism. Golden transcripts should cover stdio initialize success/failure, initialized notification, tools/list, oracle_capabilities, unknown tool, structured success, structured error, and HTTP /mcp initialize, protected-resource metadata, unauthorized WWW-Authenticate, Host/Origin guards, JSON response mode, and Streamable HTTP session behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Golden tests pass against the current rmcp/axum implementation before transport rewrite; the same tests pass after native stdio and HTTP land; fixture updates require explicit review as intentional protocol changes; snapshots contain no real secrets, PII, or sensitive business data.
notes: Testing notes: normalize timestamps/session ids/trace ids and only reorder fields where the protocol permits. Fixtures must contain no real secrets, PII, or sensitive business data. Regenerating fixtures is not a valid acceptance shortcut: changed transcript output must be reviewed as an intentional protocol change. This bead blocks W8 and W9 because native transports must prove compatibility against these artifacts.
close_reason: Added reviewed HTTP and stdio golden behavior harnesses, scrubbed fixtures, provenance notes, and shared support. Verified fmt, clippy, workspace tests, cargo deny, sensitive-data lint, and boundary lint.

title: W10: enforce HTTP OAuth scopes at dispatch
description: Fix the current captured-only ScopeGrant limitation. The HTTP path currently validates OAuth scopes but dispatch does not read them, so narrow tokens may authenticate without lowering authority. Carry a typed ScopeGrant through the native HTTP request/session context and apply monotone-down scope logic before tool dispatch. The effective operating level is the minimum of OAuth scope ceiling, profile max_level, and current session/step-up window. Scopes must never raise profile/session authority; protected profiles remain READ_ONLY even with broader tokens.
design: Implementation shape: fix the known HTTP security bug where ScopeGrant is validated but not enforced by dispatch. Carry ScopeGrant or equivalent through native HTTP request/session context and apply monotone-down logic before tool dispatch. Effective operating level is min(OAuth scope ceiling, profile max_level, current session/step-up window). Scopes can only lower authority; they never raise profile/session authority.
acceptance_criteria: A narrow read token cannot call higher-level tools; a broad token cannot exceed profile max_level; broad token plus protected profile remains READ_ONLY; missing token fails when OAuth is enabled; metadata route remains discoverable; no bearer token content is logged; the old captured-only regression is replaced by enforced least privilege.
notes: Tests: narrow oracle:read token can call read tools but cannot reach write/DDL/admin/challenge-confirmed paths if exposed; broad token cannot exceed profile max_level; broad token plus protected profile remains READ_ONLY; missing token fails when OAuth is enabled; metadata route remains discoverable; diagnostics may show resolved effective level but never token content.
close_reason: Implemented HTTP ScopeGrant dispatch context and monotone-down session-level enforcement. Verified with focused OAuth scope tests, golden HTTP tests, fmt, clippy, workspace tests, cargo deny, sensitive-data lint, boundary lint, release preflight, and cargo package.

title: W11: build deterministic Asupersync test suite
description: Prove the migration adopted Asupersync semantics rather than merely compiling. Convert async tests incrementally, not via bulk regex. Use Asupersync test helpers and LabRuntime for shutdown waiters, admission permits, request cancellation, timeout loser drain, HTTP request regions, DB call cancellation, and preview-token single-use races. Add oracles for quiescence, obligation leaks, loser drain, and futurelock where supported. Live DB tests cover connect, ping, describe, queries, binds, transactions, DBMS_OUTPUT, call timeout, LOB capping, and query cancellation cleanup. Add a negative safety test proving malformed/unauthorized SQL returns guard/classification error before network I/O, DNS, connection, lease, or mutable execution state.
design: Implementation shape: turn runtime semantics into normal tests, not optional polish. Convert async tests incrementally to Asupersync helpers. Use LabRuntime where scheduling matters: shutdown waiters, admission permits, request cancellation, timeout loser drain, HTTP request regions, DB call cancellation, and preview-token single-use races. Add oracles for quiescence, obligation leaks, loser drain, and futurelock where available.
acceptance_criteria: Normal tests pass without Tokio; at least one deterministic test proves tool-call cancellation cleanup; at least one deterministic test proves request/tool region quiescence; preview-token race yields exactly one success; guard-before-I/O negative test passes; live DB tests pass when Oracle is available and skip with explicit reason otherwise; protocol and redaction tests run in the normal suite.
notes: Required coverage: live DB tests for connect, ping, describe, query rows, named/positional binds, transaction rollback/commit, DBMS_OUTPUT, call timeout, LOB capping, query cancellation cleanup; protocol tests from W8.5; redaction tests for HTTP logging and Authorization headers; safety negative test proving malformed/unauthorized SQL returns guard/classification before network I/O, DNS, connection, lease, or mutable execution state; race test where two regions redeem one preview token and exactly one succeeds.
close_reason: Implemented deterministic Asupersync test coverage: cancelled tool-call quiescence, preview-token race one-shot success, guard-before-I/O refusal, OAuth bad-token redaction, and live-XE DBMS_OUTPUT/cancellation skip-or-pass tests. Verified with fmt, diff check, sensitive-data lint, boundary lint, clippy, workspace tests, cargo deny, and live-xe test target.

title: W12: hard gate forbidden production dependencies
description: Turn architecture decisions into automated CI invariants. Update cargo deny or a dedicated gate plus scripts/oraclemcp_boundary_lint.sh so production builds fail if Tokio, asupersync-tokio-compat, rmcp, axum, hyper, hyper-util, oracle, odpic-sys, r2d2, or other removed crate families appear in the normal dependency graph. Check normal and all-target graphs carefully so dev-only comparison tools cannot enter published artifacts. Any temporary compat callsite must carry a COMPAT-REMOVE marker tied to an open bead; final production graph fails if compat remains.
design: Implementation shape: encode the architecture as CI. Update cargo deny and/or scripts/oraclemcp_boundary_lint.sh to inspect normal production dependency graphs and fail if Tokio, tokio-stream, tokio-util, asupersync-tokio-compat, rmcp, axum, hyper, hyper-util, oracle, odpic-sys, r2d2, or removed crate families remain. Check normal and all-target graphs separately so dev-only comparison helpers are visible but cannot leak into releases. User value: future changes cannot accidentally undo the thin-native migration after it lands.
acceptance_criteria: CI fails on forbidden production dependencies; cargo deny check or the chosen gate is part of pre-commit/pre-publish gates; dev-only exceptions are documented and cannot enter published artifacts; cargo tree normal checks for each forbidden crate are visible in CI logs.
notes: Suggested visible checks: cargo tree -e normal -i tokio/asupersync-tokio-compat/rmcp/axum/hyper/hyper-util/oracle/odpic-sys/r2d2. Prefer deny.toml bans backed by explicit cargo tree CI output. Every temporary compat callsite must carry COMPAT-REMOVE tied to an open bead; final production graph fails if compat remains. Credential/logging: dependency gates should print package names and feature paths only, never environment variables or registry credentials.
close_reason: Made scripts/oraclemcp_boundary_lint.sh a hard thin-native forbidden production dependency gate, added all-target visibility and compat marker checks, wired it into release_preflight, and verified fmt, shellcheck, clippy, workspace/all-target tests, cargo deny, docs, release preflight, sensitive-data lint, and cargo package.

title: W13: measure performance and footprint evidence
description: Replace assumptions about thin-native benefits with measured evidence. Compare before/after binary size, Docker image size, install/build complexity, cold startup to first tools/list, first connection time, simple query latency, paginated query latency, concurrent tool calls under admission control, and shutdown drain under active request. Store benchmark commands, environment assumptions, and raw artifacts. Use criterion or focused integration benchmarks where they fit; do not overbuild benchmarking infrastructure.
design: Implementation shape: measure the migration outcome before making public claims. Compare against W0 baseline: binary size, Docker image size, install/build complexity, cold startup to first tools/list, first connection time, simple query latency, paginated query latency, concurrent tool calls under admission control, and shutdown drain under active request. User value: users get honest evidence for install/runtime improvements instead of marketing claims.
acceptance_criteria: README/release-note claims are backed by measured artifacts or removed; at least one benchmark proves the common read-query workflow does not unexpectedly regress; Docker image changes are measured if Docker remains a release artifact; benchmark commands and environment assumptions are documented.
notes: Evidence discipline: do not invent performance numbers. Store commands, environment assumptions, raw artifacts, and interpretation. Use criterion or focused integration benchmarks only where they fit the repo. If Docker remains a release artifact, image-size comparison is required. README/release notes must remove any claim not backed by artifacts. Credential/logging: benchmark configs must use synthetic or redacted profile data; published artifacts must not include Oracle hostnames, usernames, wallet paths, credentials, customer schema names, or sensitive query text.
close_reason: Measured thin-native footprint and performance evidence, fixed Docker builder gcc requirement and CLI broken-pipe output behavior, documented artifacts under docs/performance-footprint.md and tests/artifacts/perf. Verified fmt, clippy, workspace/all-target tests, cargo deny, boundary lint, release preflight, sensitive-data lint, docs, Docker build/runtime smoke, package generation, and diff check.

title: W14: update synchronized release artifacts
description: Ensure published artifacts match thin-native architecture. Update README, robot-docs, server.json, crates.io metadata, GHCR Dockerfile/workflow, GitHub release workflow, Docker smoke, MCP registry smoke, and release notes. Remove thick-mode/Instant Client install paths, ODPI-C build assumptions, and stable/MSRV claims where invalid. Determine the version bump before first thin-native release; this is not a patch release because removing thick mode, switching to pinned nightly, and changing Docker/source-build assumptions are breaking. Document rollback/yank recovery for immutable crates.io versions and synchronize crates.io, GitHub release, GHCR, and MCP registry by tag-driven process.
design: Implementation shape: align every outward-facing artifact with thin-native architecture. Update README, robot-docs, server.json, crates.io metadata, GHCR Dockerfile/workflow, GitHub release workflow, Docker smoke, MCP registry smoke, and release notes. Remove thick-mode install path, Instant Client requirements, ODPI-C build assumptions, and invalid stable/MSRV claims. Update profile examples and troubleshooting for thin mode. User value: installation, registry, Docker, and release instructions all match the actual binary users receive.
acceptance_criteria: cargo package --workspace succeeds; release artifacts do not document or require Instant Client; GHCR image no longer bundles Instant Client; MCP registry points to thin-native setup; release workflow gates fmt, clippy, tests, deny, forbidden-dependency lint, packaging, Docker smoke, and MCP registry smoke; crates.io/GitHub/GHCR/MCP registry are synchronized by existing tag-driven process.
notes: Release/process: decide semver before first thin-native crates.io release; this must not be a patch release because thick mode/Instant Client/stable assumptions are breaking. Release workflow gates include fmt, clippy, test, deny, forbidden dependency gate, cargo package, Docker smoke, and MCP registry smoke. Document immutable crates.io recovery: bad versions can be yanked, not unpublished; recovery is yank plus fixed release, and GHCR/GitHub/MCP registry advance to the fixed version. Credential/logging: release workflows must use GitHub secrets/OIDC without printing crates.io, GHCR, GitHub, MCP registry, Oracle, wallet, or OAuth credentials.
close_reason: Aligned thin-native release artifacts for 0.3.0: workspace/server.json/README/changelog/golden fixtures, release/GHCR/MCP registry workflows, Docker OCI label/smoke, release preflight changelog check, and package/perf docs. Verified fmt, clippy, workspace/all-target tests, cargo deny, docs, cargo package --allow-dirty, boundary lint, release preflight local+v0.3.0, sensitive-data lint, shellcheck, workflow YAML parse, negative v0.2.1 preflight, Docker build/runtime smoke, JSON parse, and diff check.

title: W2: pin nightly toolchain and CI ratchet
description: Move the workspace honestly to the pinned nightly required by oracledb/asupersync. Add rust-toolchain.toml, update CI and release workflows to use the pin, and update README/MSRV wording so the thin-native line no longer claims stable Rust/MSRV 1.88 support. Add an advisory dependency check that reports forbidden crates early without failing until W12.
design: Implementation shape: make the toolchain contract honest before adopting oracledb/asupersync. Add rust-toolchain.toml pinned to the selected nightly, update CI/release workflows to use that pin, and update docs so the thin-native line no longer claims stable/MSRV 1.88 support. The current local thin driver pin was nightly-2026-05-11 unless W0 chooses a newer deliberate pin. User value: contributors and release automation stop getting false stable-Rust signals before the migration depends on nightly-only libraries.
acceptance_criteria: cargo fmt, clippy, and tests run on the pinned nightly in CI; documentation accurately says the thin-native line is nightly-bound where it depends on asupersync/oracledb; stable/MSRV claims are removed or qualified; advisory forbidden-dependency reporting exists but is not yet hard-failing.
notes: Testing/gates: fmt, clippy, test, and deny should run under pinned nightly. Add advisory forbidden-dependency reporting now, but do not hard fail until W12 removes holdouts. This bead unlocks W3 and W6a; do not wait for DB adapter work before enabling pure server Cx propagation. Credential/logging: CI changes must not print crates.io, GitHub, GHCR, MCP registry, Oracle, wallet, or OAuth secrets; use masked GitHub secrets and avoid echoing environment values.
close_reason: Pinned nightly toolchain, removed false stable MSRV metadata/docs, updated CI/release/docker/MCP workflows, and added non-failing forbidden dependency advisory report.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: W2 verification: rust-toolchain.toml pins nightly-2026-05-11; Cargo package metadata now has no rust_version; CI/release/docker/MCP workflows use ORACLEMCP_RUST_TOOLCHAIN. Advisory dependency lint reports default and live-db forbidden holdouts without failing until W12. Local gates passed: cargo metadata, cargo fmt --all -- --check, cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test --workspace --all-targets, cargo test --workspace --doc, cargo doc --workspace --no-deps, cargo build -p oraclemcp --features live-db, cargo deny check, release_preflight, sensitive_data_lint, boundary lint, cargo package --workspace --locked --allow-dirty, PyYAML workflow parse.

title: W3: decide and prepare published oracledb dependency
description: Choose the publishable dependency path for oraclemcp. Audit crates.io oracledb 0.1.0 versus the local rust-oracledb 0.1.1+ APIs needed by W4. If required APIs are not published, publish oracledb first or document a deliberate vendoring decision. Never ship released oraclemcp crates with a local path dependency outside the workspace. Review oracledb API changes against the last published crate and document breaking changes according to semver so downstream oracledb users are not surprised.
design: Implementation shape: decide how oraclemcp will consume the pure-Rust thin driver in a publishable way. Compare crates.io oracledb 0.1.0 with local /home/durakovic/projects/rust-oracledb 0.1.1+ APIs required by W4. Prefer a published oracledb crate version; local path dependencies are allowed only for local implementation and must not ship in released oraclemcp crates. User value: releases stay reproducible from public sources and do not depend on a private checkout.
acceptance_criteria: The required oracledb version is explicit; cargo package --workspace can succeed without unpublished local path dependencies; any oracledb publish/vendoring decision includes semver notes, security-fix flow, ownership, and downstream impact.
notes: Release notes: if publishing oracledb first, review its API changes against the last published version and document semver impact for downstream oracledb users. If vendoring is chosen, document why, how versioning works, how security fixes flow, and how package ownership stays clear. Acceptance requires cargo package --workspace to work without unpublished external path deps. Credential/logging: never print cargo registry tokens; use dry-run/package checks where possible before any permanent crates.io action.
close_reason: Selected published oracledb 0.1.1; documented no-vendoring/security flow; package gate uses workspace tarball assembly plus dependency-order publish dry-runs

title: W4: replace thick ODPI-C adapter with thin oracledb adapter
description: Replace RustOracleConnection, OraclePool, ODPI-C oracle crate usage, r2d2, and spawn_blocking DB calls with a pure-Rust thin adapter based on oracledb and Asupersync-native session management. Preserve session leases, rollback-on-release/expiry, DBMS_OUTPUT, temp tables, package globals, transactions, savepoints, bind/result type fidelity, NUMBER-as-string by default, NLS-stable serialization, AWR/ASH license gating, Statspack/unavailable degradation, call timeouts, and existing tool contracts. Audit proxy auth, ADB wallet/TCPS/SNI, OCI IAM, wallet passwords, Kerberos/RADIUS/SEPS, login/trusted statements, session identity, edition, standby caps, and every profile key. Unsupported modes must fail explicitly and show in doctor. If a mission-critical current auth path is unsupported, removing thick mode is blocked until the operator chooses upstreaming, scoping, or pausing.
design: Implementation shape: replace RustOracleConnection/OraclePool/ODPI-C oracle/r2d2/spawn_blocking with an oracledb-backed thin adapter and Asupersync-native session manager. Preserve stateful session lease semantics: DBMS_OUTPUT, temp tables, package globals, transactions, savepoints, commit/rollback, and session state must stay pinned to the same physical Oracle session. Stateless clean reads may use any clean connection. Cancellation must discard or clean dirty sessions.
acceptance_criteria: Default build needs no ODPI-C/Instant Client; live DB tests pass through thin mode where configured; every DB tool works or returns a documented structured unsupported-feature error; oracle/r2d2/spawn_blocking are gone from production DB paths; session lease and type-fidelity tests pass; complete structured driver errors are redacted before logs/MCP/errors; unsupported auth is visible in doctor.
notes: Detailed checks: map query_rows, query_rows_named, execute, commit, rollback, describe, ping, call_timeout, set_call_timeout, enable/read DBMS_OUTPUT; map binds for strings, numbers, nulls, booleans, dates/timestamps, binary/LOBs, named/positional binds; preserve Oracle NUMBER/high-precision decimal as lossless JSON string by default; preserve DATE/TIMESTAMP/INTERVAL/RAW/ROWID behavior and NLS independence. Audit external auth, username/password, proxy CONNECT THROUGH, TCPS, wallet location/password, OCI IAM token, Kerberos/RADIUS/SEPS, session identity, driver name, edition, login/trusted statements, standby caps. Redact complete structured driver errors before MCP/log conversion. If a mission-critical current auth path is unsupported, do not remove thick mode until the operator chooses upstreaming, scoping, or pausing.
close_reason: Implemented thin oracledb adapter; removed oracle/r2d2/Tokio DB path; unsupported DBMS_OUTPUT OUT bind, external wallet auth, IAM token auth, and edition selection are structured errors with upstream rust-oracledb beads rust-oracledb-acj/o0b/5bh/jr9 filed. Gates: cargo fmt --all -- --check; cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo test --workspace; cargo test -p oraclemcp-db --features live-xe --test live_oracle -- --nocapture (7 skip/pass due invalid local creds); cargo clippy -p oraclemcp-db --features live-xe --test live_oracle -- -D warnings; cargo deny check; boundary lint; release_preflight; sensitive_data_lint; cargo package --workspace --locked --allow-dirty --no-verify.

title: W5: update thin-mode doctor, docs, and credential safety
description: Make thin mode understandable and safe for humans and agents. Update doctor to diagnose DNS, TCP, service/SID, auth, server version, current schema, role/open mode, standby status, TCPS handshake, wallet readability/format, session identity, and privileges. Remove Instant Client as a required diagnostic and rewrite README/robot-docs quickstart around thin setup. Keep ORA codes scrapeable and structured for agent RCA, distinguish listener/network from auth/wallet/TCPS/unsupported-feature failures, and add redaction tests for connect options, OAuth debug output, config errors, doctor output, failed connection output, wallet fields, fixtures, PII, and sensitive business data.
design: Implementation shape: make thin setup self-diagnosing for humans and agents. Doctor should test/report DNS, TCP connect, service/SID reachability, authentication, server version, current schema, role/open mode, read-only standby, TCPS handshake, wallet readability/format, session identity, privileges, unsupported auth modes, and ORA-code classification. Remove Instant Client from required diagnostics while explaining thin mode does not need it.
acceptance_criteria: doctor no longer tells thin-native users to install Instant Client; common setup failures have actionable next steps; ORA codes and failure classes are structured; no fixture/snapshot/log/doctor/error output contains real-looking secrets, PII, or sensitive business data; robot docs explain thin setup and recovery.
notes: Docs/tests: update README quickstart, robot-docs, troubleshooting, Docker story, and profile examples. Keep ORA codes scrapeable and structured in doctor and MCP error envelopes so agents can choose retry/introspection/setup repair. Add redaction tests for OracleConnectOptions, thin ConnectOptions, OAuth debug, config errors, doctor output, failed connection output, wallet fields, fixtures, PII, and sensitive business data.
close_reason: Implemented thin-native doctor/docs hardening: removed Instant Client diagnostics, redacted connect material and wallet paths, added structured failure_class/ora_code fields, updated robot docs/README, and verified with fmt, clippy, tests, deny, sensitive-data lint, boundary lint, release preflight, package, and doctor smokes.

title: W6a: propagate Cx through pure server dispatch
description: Introduce the pure server-side async trait and &Cx shape after the nightly pin, without waiting for thin DB adapter work. Replace synchronous ToolDispatch::dispatch with an async Cx-aware shape at the server boundary, update registry/core/custom-tool/session-tool call paths that do not require DB adapter readiness, keep pure guard/classifier logic pure, preserve ErrorEnvelope and oracle_capabilities, and avoid ambient runtime handles.
design: Implementation shape: introduce async &Cx-aware dispatch for pure server layers after W2, before W4. Replace synchronous ToolDispatch::dispatch shape with async fn dispatch(&self, cx: &Cx, name: &str, args: Value) -> Result<Value, ErrorEnvelope> or an equivalent local pattern. Update registry/core/custom-tools/session-tool call paths that do not require thin DB adapter readiness. Keep pure guard/classifier code pure where no Cx is needed. User value: transport/runtime migration can proceed in parallel with DB adapter work without mixing unrelated risks.
acceptance_criteria: Pure server dispatch APIs accept &Cx; stdio/runtime transport work can compile against the new shape; no pure server async path depends on ambient Tokio runtime handles; guard/classifier code remains pure where appropriate; focused cargo check/tests cover the migrated pure dispatch slice.
notes: Implemented Cx-aware ToolDispatch boundary locally and verified focused server/dispatcher/e2e tests, cargo fmt, clippy, workspace tests, and boundary lint. Blocked before commit because cargo deny rejects asupersync 0.3.4/asupersync-macros/franken-* license metadata (LicenseRef-MIT-OpenAI-Anthropic-Rider). Do not land until oraclemcp-asupersync-license-policy-g7a is resolved by explicit owner/legal policy decision, permissively licensed fork/relicense, or runtime plan change.
close_reason: Implemented Cx-aware object-safe ToolDispatch seam, explicit run_tool_with_cx path, OracleDispatcher Cx checkpoint, updated harness dispatchers, and deterministic stdio golden driver. Verified fmt, clippy, workspace tests, cargo deny.

title: W6b: propagate Cx through DB-facing tool execution
description: After the thin adapter exists, pass &Cx through DB-backed tool execution and cancellation-sensitive paths. Enforce the critical safety rule: raw SQL classification and level gating must complete before any await, DB connection acquisition, lease acquisition, or mutable execution-side state transition. Re-check preview and execute phases independently. Add checkpoints in long serialization, pagination, retry, shutdown-sensitive, and async custom-tool validation loops. Cancelled DB calls must cancel/abandon thin-driver work, rollback leased transactions, close cursors, or discard dirty connections.
design: Implementation shape: after W4, propagate &Cx into DB-backed tools and thin adapter calls. Enforce fail-closed ordering: raw SQL classification and operating-level gating must complete before any await, DNS/network I/O, DB connection acquisition, session lease acquisition, or execution-side mutable state transition. Preview and execute phases both re-check the exact SQL/object/profile/options for that phase. User value: async adoption improves cancellation without opening a TOCTOU hole in the SQL guard.
acceptance_criteria: Individual DB-backed tools pass &Cx to the thin adapter; guard tests prove no await/connection/lease boundary runs before classification/level gating; cancellation can be tested at the tool-call boundary; dirty sessions are rolled back or discarded instead of returned cleanly.
notes: Cancellation policy: add checkpoints in long serialization, paginated fetch, retry, shutdown-sensitive, and async custom-tool validation loops. Cancelled DB calls must cancel/abandon driver work, roll back leased transactions, close cursors, or discard dirty connections. Tests must prove no pre-classification await/lease boundary and that cancellation cannot return a dirty session to the pool. Credential/logging: DB tool errors must pass through the same redaction path as W4; do not log raw bind values, passwords, IAM tokens, wallet passwords, or bearer tokens.
close_reason: Implemented Cx propagation through DB dispatch, cancellation checkpoints, dirty-session rollback tests, and full local gates.

title: W7a: replace Tokio runtime bootstrap and pure primitives
description: Replace the binary runtime bootstrap and pure runtime primitives with Asupersync. Move startup to RuntimeBuilder, audit telemetry for Tokio-tied layers, replace shutdown Notify, admission Semaphore, timeout logic, signal handling, TCP listener usage, tokio tests, and tokio::io test transports where the paths are not DB-adapter dependent. Define shutdown drain policy: stop admissions, cancel/drain active request regions, flush audit sinks where possible, and enforce a hard configurable drain timeout.
design: Implementation shape: replace pure Tokio runtime surfaces with Asupersync primitives. Move binary bootstrap to RuntimeBuilder, replace Notify, Semaphore/admission, timeout, signal, TCP listener, tokio tests, and tokio::io test transports where DB adapter readiness is not needed. Define shutdown drain: stop new admissions, cancel/drain active request/tool regions, flush audit sinks where possible, and enforce a hard operator-configurable drain timeout.
acceptance_criteria: Direct tokio imports are gone from pure production runtime/server paths; tests cover shutdown waiters, lost wakeups, admission caps, timeout losers, signal/drain behavior, and new-work refusal during drain; any temporary compat module is isolated with a removal bead; telemetry does not reintroduce Tokio.
notes: Safety notes: audit tracing/telemetry for Tokio-tied layers and prevent reintroduction. Audit unwrap/expect in credential, connection, OAuth, HMAC, wallet, and token-handling paths; replace panic-prone handling with structured errors where sensitive values may be in scope and verify panic hooks redact. Any temporary compat module must be isolated with a removal bead and cannot survive W12.
close_reason: Replaced pure runtime primitives with Asupersync Notify/Semaphore/timeout, added drain refusal, converted targeted tests, and marked rmcp/axum Tokio holdouts as W8/W9 compatibility boundaries.

title: W7b: wire Asupersync cancellation into DB cleanup
description: Complete the DB-specific part of W7 once W6b exists. DB calls must receive cancellation through Asupersync child regions/budgets, and cancelled or timed-out calls must leave no reusable dirty session. Leased sessions with open transactions must rollback before reuse; open cursors must close or force connection discard; timeout errors must stay structured and actionable.
design: Implementation shape: complete DB-specific Asupersync cancellation after W6b. DB calls should be controlled by child regions/budgets and integrate with thin driver timeout/cancel behavior. Cancellation or timeout must leave no reusable dirty session: open transactions roll back, open cursors close, and uncertain sessions are discarded rather than returned to the clean pool. User value: operators can interrupt/shut down safely without corrupting pooled Oracle session state.
acceptance_criteria: A deterministic or focused integration test proves DB tool cancellation cleanup; cancelled DB work cannot return a dirty connection to the pool; rollback/close/discard behavior is documented; timeout/cancellation errors map cleanly to ErrorEnvelope/MCP surfaces.
notes: Testing: add deterministic or focused integration tests for cancellation during query/fetch, cancellation during leased transaction, timeout loser drain, and rollback/close/discard decision. Error surfaces must stay structured and actionable through ErrorEnvelope and MCP responses. Credential/logging: cancellation and timeout logs should include safe correlation/session identifiers only, never connect strings, bind values, usernames with embedded proxy targets if sensitive, wallet paths when policy treats paths as sensitive, or token material.
close_reason: Implemented Cx-aware lease preview cleanup, pool cancellation discard policy, timeout envelope coverage, docs, and full local gates.

title: W8: implement native stdio MCP transport
description: Remove rmcp from the default local-agent stdio path while preserving MCP client compatibility. Implement JSON-RPC envelope parsing/writing for initialize, notifications/initialized, tools/list, tools/call, oracle_capabilities, init-token validation, structured success/error results, and unknown tool failure. Audit protocol-type-only crates such as tower-mcp-types or rust-mcp-schema, or use internal serde structs if dependencies/version mismatch. Reject invalid JSON, unknown methods, invalid params, and oversized messages fail-closed. Init tokens require timing-safe comparison; logs must stay structured and non-secret.
design: Implementation shape: implement native JSON-RPC stdio MCP transport without rmcp. Preserve initialize, initialized notification, tools/list, tools/call, oracle_capabilities, init-token validation, structured success/error results, and unknown-tool failure. Decide whether protocol-type-only crates are acceptable after auditing dependencies and MCP version fit; use internal serde structs if that is cleaner.
acceptance_criteria: Golden stdio transcripts pass; e2e_stdio.rs and other stdio tests are ported away from rmcp helpers and pass against native implementation; initialize protocolVersion exactly matches advertised version; rmcp no longer appears in the stdio production path; at least one real or faithful scripted MCP client invocation verifies compatibility.
notes: Protocol/tests: invalid JSON returns parse error, unknown method returns method-not-found or protocol-appropriate error, invalid params fail closed, oversized messages reject before JSON parsing, ids echo correctly, notifications do not receive responses. Init tokens require timing-safe comparison. Port e2e_stdio.rs and other stdio tests away from rmcp helpers. Acceptance includes a faithful scripted or real MCP client invocation.
close_reason: Implemented native blocking JSON-RPC stdio transport with explicit Asupersync dispatch, kept transcripts stable, moved rmcp/Tokio runtime to HTTP only, and ported stdio tests off rmcp/Tokio.

title: W8.5: add MCP conformance tests
description: Prove the native MCP core is spec-shaped, not just a replay of old SDK happy paths. Record the MCP spec version under test; verify protocol negotiation, malformed JSON, unknown methods, invalid params, notifications, id echoing, batch behavior implemented or explicitly rejected, tools/list schema shape, unadvertised tool rejection, and structured content compatibility. Reuse the suite for HTTP where transport differences allow.
design: Implementation shape: build protocol conformance tests for the native MCP core after W8 and before W9. Record the MCP spec version under test. Test version negotiation, malformed JSON, unknown method, invalid params, notification no-response behavior, id echoing, batch support implemented or explicitly rejected, tools/list capability/schema shape, tools/call rejecting unadvertised names, and structured content compatibility.
acceptance_criteria: Native stdio passes conformance before W9 starts; deviations from MCP spec are documented as deliberate compatibility choices; the conformance suite is permanent and later runs against HTTP too.
notes: Use the same suite for HTTP in W9 where transport differences allow. Document any intentional spec deviations as compatibility choices with current clients. This bead gates W9 so HTTP does not inherit an unproven MCP core. Credential/logging: conformance fixtures should use synthetic tokens, synthetic profile names, and synthetic request ids only; tests must assert protocol errors do not echo secret-bearing inputs.
close_reason: Added native stdio MCP conformance harness covering initialize, notifications, tools/list, tools/call, JSON-RPC errors, oversized/batch rejection, id echoing, and token non-echo; documented coverage and accepted deviations.

title: [B6e] Finish the http de-monolith: extract the SSE/stream cluster, then triage the 9.6k-line inline test module
description:
Beads .1-.4 are done (serve 6213b15, config a2ed5f2, stores ecde279, operator 12fb3c2). crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs is now 2904 lines, down from the 11,495-line god-file the epic opened against. Two steps from the EPIC's own plan never got a bead:

  (1) sse.rs — the epic's step 4. sse_writer.rs already holds the low-level frame writers, but HttpSseStream, HttpToolStream + its binding/notifications, sse_response, buffered_sse_response and the stream-cursor helpers are still in mod.rs (~450 lines). Same recipe as the four that landed: verbatim move, 'use super::*', pub(super) for whatever crosses the wall, then the gate (per-BINARY pass counts vs baseline, pub-item diff empty, clippy -D warnings, and the 52 security substrings still inside http/*.rs).

  (2) http/tests.rs is 9,651 lines — a B9 test monolith, now 3.3x the size of the module it tests. It is ALSO the contract: concurrency_contract.rs include_str!s it at src/http/tests.rs, and dashboard_e2e's scanner concatenates it. Splitting it needs its own reviewed bead because the include_str! path is a compile-time pin; a split must either keep tests.rs as a facade that includes the parts, or update that pin in the same commit.

HARD CONSTRAINT for whoever takes this, learned the expensive way in .4: dashboard_e2e::read_http_source does a NON-RECURSIVE read_dir over crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/*.rs. Any submodule placed in a SUBDIRECTORY silently escapes that security-substring scan - the test keeps passing while scanning less. Keep new http submodules FLAT.
close_reason:
SSE/streaming surface extracted in commit 43ba7ca. crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs 2904 -> 2348 lines; new FLAT crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/sse.rs (594 lines).

WHAT MOVED (one contiguous block, mod.rs 2241-2808, verbatim): the stream-cursor helpers (stream_event_sequence, parse_stream_cursor, validate_stream_cursor_binding, events_after_sequence), HttpSseStream + impl, HttpToolStream + HttpToolStreamBinding + HttpToolStreamNotifications + impl, tool_stream_frame_data, write_tool_stream_event_buffered/chunked, buffered_sse_response, streaming_query_chunks, append_nonstreaming_response_if_session, retain_server_notifications, SseResponseEvents and sse_response. Same recipe as the four that landed: `use super::*`, pub(super) for exactly what crosses the wall, nothing else widened. The SSE consts (SSE_KEEPALIVE_INTERVAL, HTTP_SSE_CAPACITY_SCOPE, QUERY_ROW_STREAM_CHANNEL_CAPACITY) deliberately stay in mod.rs where the route handlers read them.

ISOMORPHISM PROVEN, not asserted:
- VERBATIM: a script reconstructed the moved text by stripping only the visibility tokens we added and compared it to `git show HEAD:mod.rs` lines 2241-2808 — byte-identical. Zero logic edits; no fail-closed default touched, relaxed, or reordered (cursor unparseable = 400, cursor bound to another session = 400 scope mismatch, cursor older than the ring = 410 or an explicit gap event, stream runs only while holding its admission permit — all moved unchanged).
- TESTS (the contract, unchanged): omcpb test -p oraclemcp-core before and after; per-binary pass counts diffed with wall-clock stripped = IDENTICAL on every binary (lib 622 passed, plus every integration binary). No test was edited.
- PUBLIC API: cargo public-api -p oraclemcp-core before vs after = EMPTY diff across 46,040 items. The facade (`mod sse;` + `use sse::{...}` + a #[cfg(test)] `use sse::streaming_query_chunks`) keeps every path resolving where it did: mod.rs body, serve.rs (which calls .write_to on both stream types), stores.rs (which reaches the four cursor helpers through its own `use super::*`), and the inline test module.
- SECURITY SCAN: the file is FLAT (http/sse.rs), so dashboard_e2e::read_http_source's NON-RECURSIVE read_dir still picks it up — verified by replicating the scanner over the real directory listing: it scans config/mod/operator/request_target/serve/sse/sse_writer/stores/tests/wire and all 59 asserted http marker/security substrings are still present, 0 missing.
- fmt clean; clippy on oraclemcp-core reports ZERO findings in src/http/*.

TWO THINGS THE ORCHESTRATOR SHOULD KNOW (not fixed by me, not mine):
1. `omcpb clippy -p oraclemcp-core -- -D warnings` currently fails inside crates/oraclemcp-db ("this method chain can be written more clearly with if..else") — another agent's in-flight edit, not http/. Clippy on my own files is clean.
2. `omcpb test -p oraclemcp --test dashboard_e2e` cannot run at all right now: crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs does not compile (E0382 partially-moved `resolved`; E0599 no method `with_cumulative_query_cost_budget`) — pane 6's in-flight P1 work. That is why the security scan above was proven by replicating read_http_source's logic over the real files rather than by running the binary; the assertion set and the directory listing are the same ones the test uses.

NOT DONE (out of this bead's scope, flagged in the bead body): http/tests.rs is still a 9,800-line B9 test monolith and needs its own reviewed bead, because concurrency_contract.rs include_str!s it at src/http/tests.rs — a compile-time pin a split must keep or update in the same commit.

title: W9: implement native HTTP MCP transport
description: Replace Axum, Hyper, and HTTP-side rmcp with Asupersync-native or minimal non-Tokio HTTP/web primitives. Preserve /mcp, loopback-safe default bind, explicit remote bind opt-in, Host guard, Origin guard, JSON response mode, Streamable HTTP stateful/session behavior, protected-resource metadata, OAuth bearer validation, and clear unauthenticated failures. Host/Origin and request-size checks must happen before OAuth/tool dispatch. Bearer tokens require timing-safe comparison. Log HTTP headers by explicit allowlist only; Authorization, Cookie, Referer, proxy auth, and deployment-specific sensitive headers must not leak by default. Each HTTP request is an Asupersync region with narrowed capabilities and owned spawned work.
design: Implementation shape: rebuild /mcp on Asupersync native HTTP/web primitives or a minimal non-Tokio HTTP library selected by W0. Preserve loopback-safe default bind, explicit remote bind opt-in, Host guard, Origin guard, JSON response mode, Streamable HTTP stateful/session behavior, protected-resource metadata, OAuth bearer validation, and clear unauthenticated failure responses. Host/Origin and size checks run before OAuth and before tool dispatch.
acceptance_criteria: Golden HTTP transcripts pass; HTTP e2e tests are ported away from axum helpers; axum/hyper/rmcp are absent from production dependency graph; Streamable HTTP remains supported; shutdown drains active request regions; host/origin/OAuth/request-size tests prove checks happen before dispatch and logs do not leak sensitive headers.
notes: Security/protocol: every HTTP request is a region; spawned work belongs to that request; shutdown drains active request regions. Capabilities should be narrowed so metadata routes do not get DB/spawn authority unless needed. Compare bearer tokens with timing-safe equality after shape/length validation. Log only explicit safe headers; Authorization, Cookie, Referer, proxy auth, and deployment-specific sensitive headers must not leak. Avoid carrying axum/tower abstractions forward mechanically.
close_reason: Implemented native HTTP MCP transport without rmcp/axum/hyper/Tokio production deps; ported HTTP e2e/goldens; preserved Streamable HTTP, guards, OAuth metadata/challenge, request-size rejection before OAuth, and shutdown drain worker join; verified fmt, clippy, workspace tests, cargo deny, diff check.

title: audit verify: tail-truncation of the JSONL chain is undetected (no head anchor / expected-length)
description:
OBSERVATION (leave-as-bead; documented residual, NOT a regression) from the 2026-07 multipass bug hunt audit-chain e2e on xe18.

WHAT WAS TESTED: ran the operating-level ladder against xe18 with ORACLEMCP_AUDIT_KEY set, produced a 15-record signed hash-chain, then ran oraclemcp audit verify against tampered copies:
- in-place edit of a record  -> DETECTED (exit 2, HashMismatch, broken_at_index correct)
- delete a middle record (splice/reorder) -> DETECTED (PrevHashMismatch)
- wrong signing key -> DETECTED (SignatureMismatch)
- DROP THE LAST RECORD (tail truncation) -> NOT DETECTED: verify reported ok:true, records:14.

ROOT CAUSE: verify_records (crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/verify.rs) re-walks from genesis checking hash-link + monotonic-seq + keyed-MAC. A prefix of a valid chain is itself a valid chain, so dropping trailing records yields a still-valid shorter chain. There is no persisted head anchor (last seq / last entry_hash) or expected-count for verify to compare against.

STATUS: this is a KNOWN, DOCUMENTED design boundary, not a new bug. docs/threat-model.md T5 honestly claims only the three checks verify actually performs (in-place edit, reorder, recompute-from-genesis) and points at optional WORM/SIEM shipping (crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/shipping.rs, ADR 0003) as the mitigation for an independent tamper-evident copy — which is exactly what catches tail-truncation. The oracle_version_matrix.sh harness also asserts .records >= 10 as a coarse floor. So the property is covered at the architecture level; verify simply cannot detect it locally by design.

SUGGESTED HARDENING (optional, non-blocking): let audit verify optionally take an expected head (--expect-seq N / --expect-head <entry_hash>) or persist a separate head-pointer file that verify cross-checks, so a local-only deployment (no shipping) can detect tail-truncation too. Alternatively, have verify emit an advisory note that trailing truncation is not locally detectable without an anchor. Do NOT change the default success semantics without an explicit anchor input (would break the honest 'a valid prefix is valid' contract).

EVIDENCE: /tmp scratch repro (env-only + file copies, no repo mutation); binary /tmp/cargo-target/debug/oraclemcp audit verify.
close_reason: Fixed: audit verify now detects tail-truncation via a signed sidecar head anchor (crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/anchor.rs, with_head_anchor on the sink). Crash-consistency: the record fsync ALWAYS precedes the anchor update, so a crash window can leave the anchor BEHIND the durable chain (explainable, self-heals on next append) but NEVER ahead; verify reports TRUNCATED when the chain ends before the anchored head. Anchor update failure fails the call closed. Record format unchanged (anchor is additive sidecar). 11 anchor unit tests (intact/truncated/anchor-behind crash-window) + ladder e2e audit step, all green; documented in docs/hardening.md.

title: [P1][live] XE 18c/21c lack DBMS_FLASHBACK: flashback-dependent features must fail with a typed, version-aware refusal
notes: Reset to open 2026-07-16: orphaned in_progress claim. Blocked by 5c7i live matrix; finish the version-aware DBMS_FLASHBACK typed refusal once the matrix establishes 18c/21c behavior.
close_reason: Premise corrected + real gap fixed (commit 64682ba). PREMISE WRONG: 18c/21c do NOT lack DBMS_FLASHBACK -- package is VALID on 18c/21c/23ai and as SYSTEM a real flashback window opens+closes on all three; the refusal is a missing EXECUTE grant on SYS.DBMS_FLASHBACK, not a version gap. Did NOT implement a literal 'wrong version' refusal: it would be false and would send operators to an upgrade instead of a grant. The typed version-aware refusal ALREADY worked end-to-end (capability_unavailable + 'database version 18.4.0.0.0'/'21.3.0.0.0', presumably 6sj8.3.2). REAL GAP FIXED: the live test skipped on any message containing 'FLASHBACK', so nothing pinned the contract and a regression to a silent/generic error would still pass. Now asserts on the typed DbError::FlashbackRefusal variant (kind + version named) and fails loudly on any flashback-shaped untyped error. ALSO fixed a pre-existing RED on 23ai (unreported): the replay hit ORA-01466 every run because the flashback target sat inside Oracle's ~3s SCN->time granularity after CREATE TABLE, so the positive path never ran; an 8s settle makes it genuinely exercise the real flashback path. Tested a hypothesis and DISCARDED it: the ORA-00904 -> V$DATABASE.CURRENT_SCN fallback is NOT a weakened path -- both SCN sources drift in lockstep inside a read-only txn (t=0 fb=11237984/vdb=11237985 -> t=8s both +6) and neither pins to the snapshot, so they are the same quantity; fallback left intact. EVIDENCE: 18c refusal asserted, 21c refusal asserted, 23ai positive replay green; cargo fmt -p oraclemcp-db clean; cargo clippy -p oraclemcp-db --features live-xe --all-targets -D warnings clean. NOT DONE (see discovered bead): lab profiles still lack the grant, so the POSITIVE replay path stays uncovered on 18c/21c.

title: Register scripts/e2e/egress.sh in run_all.sh (blocked on time_diff.sh landing)
description:
The governed-egress matrix (bead .4.6) landed its test + script in 1d6a37d, but the one-line registration in scripts/e2e/run_all.sh is NOT committed.

WHY IT COULD NOT LAND: run_all.sh in the working tree carries another agent's uncommitted line adding scripts/e2e/time_diff.sh, and that script is still untracked. run_all.sh dispatches each scenario with bash "$script" and has no existence check, so committing run_all.sh in that state would commit a reference to a file absent from HEAD and make run_all report a phantom failure for every consumer. Landing a file that references uncommitted symbols/files is the exact broken-HEAD failure the orchestrator called out.

CURRENT STATE: the line 'scripts/e2e/egress.sh' is staged non-destructively in the working-tree copy of run_all.sh (the other agent's line preserved). egress.sh IS tracked at HEAD (1d6a37d), so it is safe for whoever lands run_all.sh next to carry both lines.

TO CLOSE: once scripts/e2e/time_diff.sh is committed, land run_all.sh (it may already carry the egress.sh line). Then verify: ./scripts/e2e/run_all.sh --dry-run --log lists egress among the dispatched scenarios.
close_reason:
VERIFIED + PROVEN LIVE. The scripts/e2e/run_all.sh line for egress.sh had already been landed by another agent once the time_diff.sh blocker cleared, so the one-line edit itself was not mine to make; what was missing was the PROOF that it actually runs. Both now exist:

  * DISPATCH PROVEN: ./scripts/e2e/run_all.sh --log --dry-run emits a scenario_dispatch event for scripts/e2e/egress.sh (16 scenarios dispatched, summary 'pass=7 fail=0 skipped=9 total=16').
  * LIVE PROVEN: ran ./scripts/e2e/egress.sh against the real matrix (XE 18c on 1518, XE 21c on 1520, FREE 23ai on 1522) - GREEN: 'governed-egress matrix green: 4 cases x 3 lanes', scenario_complete/pass. The four cases (default-masked unconfigured column, tokenized self-join leaking no plaintext, mask-certificate re-derivation, and the fourth case) all pass on every lane. egress.sh drives cargo test -p oraclemcp-db --test live_egress, so it did not depend on the oraclemcp binary that another agent currently has mid-edit.
  * COVERAGE.md: added the governed-egress row, which was missing.

Also landed the systemic fix so this class of bug cannot recur: e2e_harness.rs::every_e2e_scenario_script_is_reachable_from_the_runner_or_its_own_test asserts that every *.sh in scripts/e2e is either dispatched by run_all.sh or carries its own harness dry-run test (the two deliberate release/operator gates are the named exceptions). Landed 141a87d.

title: Nightly requirement is misattributed: it comes from oracledb pulling asupersync's DEFAULT nightly-outcome-try, not from asupersync 'requiring' nightly
description:
[HIGH — claim vs reality] PHASE-4 reality check. README.md, AGENTS.md, Cargo.toml:22 and rust-toolchain.toml all state the thin-native line 'has no stable MSRV because asupersync 0.3.5 requires nightly-only language features (try_trait_v2 + try_trait_v2_residual)'. That attribution is WRONG, and it is load-bearing: it is why we tell users to install a pinned nightly and why we advertise no stable MSRV.

TRACED ROOT CAUSE (each step verified, not inferred):
1. asupersync 0.3.5 does NOT require nightly. Its only nightly use is CONDITIONAL: src/lib.rs:52 = #![cfg_attr(feature = "nightly-outcome-try", feature(try_trait_v2))]. (A plain grep for '#![feature(' finds nothing — the cfg_attr is why this went unnoticed.)
2. asupersync's default feature set = ["proc-macros", "nightly-outcome-try"] — so the nightly feature is ON BY DEFAULT but OPT-OUTABLE.
3. oraclemcp does it RIGHT: Cargo.toml:35 = asupersync { default-features = false, features = ["metrics"] }. We never ask for nightly.
4. oracledb 0.8.3 does NOT: its [dependencies.asupersync] declares version = "=0.3.5", features = ["tls"] with NO default-features = false. It therefore pulls asupersync's defaults, and cargo feature unification turns nightly-outcome-try ON for the whole graph — overriding our opt-out.
5. NEITHER repo ever requests nightly-outcome-try (grep: only comments mention it). The nightly feature appears to be entirely accidental.
6. Driver 0.8.4's own Cargo.toml:13-14 already states this accurately ('asupersync's DEFAULT nightly-outcome-try feature enables #![feature(try_trait_v2)]') — but documents the consequence instead of turning it off. oraclemcp's docs are the ones that misattribute it to asupersync 'requiring' nightly.

EVIDENCE THAT STABLE IS OTHERWISE FINE: cargo +stable check -p oraclemcp-error -p oraclemcp-guard = EXIT 0 on stable rustc 1.96.0. cargo +stable check -p asupersync fails ONLY with E0554 pointing at that cfg_attr line.

HYPOTHESIS TO TEST (explicitly NOT yet proven — do not close on reading alone): setting default-features = false on oracledb's asupersync dep retires the nightly requirement for BOTH repos. It must keep proc-macros, which is asupersync's other default and is probably genuinely used: i.e. asupersync = { version = "=0.3.5", default-features = false, features = ["tls", "proc-macros"] }. Test by patching the driver locally and running cargo +stable check on the driver, then on the server. If something DOES need Outcome's  (try_trait_v2), the requirement is real — and then the docs should say 'we opt into asupersync's nightly-outcome-try because X', not 'asupersync requires nightly'.

WHY IT MATTERS: 'no stable MSRV' is a significant adoption barrier stated as an inherent property of a dependency. If it is really an unrequested default feature, we are imposing nightly on every downstream user for nothing. Note tzju ('0.3.9 may retire nightly requirement') assumes the fix rides on the 0.3.9 bump — this analysis suggests the fix may be independent of 0.3.9 and available today.

SCOPE: the one-line dep change lives in the DRIVER repo (rust-oracledb), so this is cross-repo and gates on driver publish. oraclemcp's own docs (README toolchain section, AGENTS.md 'Rust toolchain & gates', Cargo.toml:22, rust-toolchain.toml) must be corrected either way — even if nightly stays, the stated reason is wrong.

DO NOT: silently drop the nightly pin without proving stable builds AND the full gate passes on stable.
close_reason:
FIXED in df41540 (docs + the gate that pinned the wrong claim). Scope was attribution, not the pin: the nightly requirement is REAL and stays.

TRUE SOURCE — verified, two INDEPENDENT reasons, neither is 'asupersync requires nightly':
1. ALL PLATFORMS: asupersync 0.3.5 gates its nightly use behind a cargo feature — asupersync-0.3.5/src/lib.rs:52-53 = #![cfg_attr(feature = "nightly-outcome-try", feature(try_trait_v2))] + the same for try_trait_v2_residual. The feature is OPT-IN, merely present in asupersync's default set (default = [proc-macros, nightly-outcome-try]). We already opt out (default-features = false). It lands anyway because oracledb declares its asupersync dep WITHOUT default-features = false, so cargo unifies it back on. PROOF: cargo tree -i asupersync -e features shows 'asupersync feature "default" <- oracledb v0.8.3'.
2. WINDOWS ONLY: our OWN oraclemcp-core/src/lib.rs:9 = #![cfg_attr(windows, feature(windows_by_handle))], needed for MetadataExt::number_of_links — file_store's hard-linked-lock refusal (bead 7oaa, the Windows counterpart of the unix nlink check from siry/em39) and the audit sink's file identity. No stable std equivalent, so Windows needs nightly even if (1) is resolved.

ANSWER TO THE BEAD'S QUESTION ('is try_trait_v2 used by OUR code or only by asupersync?'): only by asupersync, and only when its nightly-outcome-try feature is on. Our code's ONLY nightly feature is the Windows windows_by_handle above. Neither the driver's nor the server's source uses the try_trait_v2 syntax — it arrives transitively and unrequested. NOTE the docs' FEATURE NAMES were right (try_trait_v2 + try_trait_v2_residual both exist, lines 52-53); only the WHY was wrong.

CORRECTED: AGENTS.md, README.md (x2), Cargo.toml, rust-toolchain.toml, docs/TOOLCHAIN.md §1 (rewritten with the exact mechanism + a cargo tree reproducer), docs/operations.md. ADR-0001 keeps its original decision text (an ADR records what was decided) and gains a dated Correction section + a status banner, because its review trigger was materially wrong: it says 'wait for asupersync to stop needing nightly', when oracledb setting default-features = false may drop the requirement TODAY with no upstream release. That is recorded as a HYPOTHESIS TO PROVE (must keep proc-macros; must build AND gate green on stable first), not a claim — I did not prove it, and the pin stays until someone does.

ROOT CAUSE OF THE PERSISTENCE (the real find): scripts/release_surface_sync_check.sh REQUIRED the docs to contain the literal string '<driver> driver is stable-clean' — the drift-guard was actively pinning the inaccurate sentence in place, which is why it survived multiple doc passes. Anchors now require the accurate 'driver's own source is stable-clean' wording. The provenance INTENT is preserved, not weakened: the anchors still interpolate the pinned versions, and I verified the gate is still load-bearing by simulating both bumps — oracledb 0.8.3->0.8.4 and asupersync 0.3.5->0.3.9 each make it fail.

NOT CHANGED: the nightly pin itself, rust-toolchain.toml's channel, or any code. Docs + one gate script only.

GATE: release_surface_sync_check EXIT 0 (version=0.9.0, surfaces=26); oraclemcp_honesty_grep EXIT 0; oraclemcp_api_lock EXIT 0; cargo metadata EXIT 0.

title: B4: pre-tag release-rehearsal workflow_dispatch (server)
description:
Source: PLAN_ENGINEERING_PROGRAM.md sec.29.3, the last undone item of Cluster B tranche 1 in THIS repo (see the triage comment on bp8ia.3: the other three are already landed).

WHY: the 0.9.0 tag took FOUR attempts. Every failure was release-mechanics, not a code bug, and each one cost a red run plus a fix commit plus a re-tag: (1) binary-size budget stale, 32MB raised to 48MB (62dff28); (2) installer-embed so the packaged tarball verifies (cfc650b); (3) release-gates job hit its 45m cap, raised to 60m (e6d80d2); (4) earlier, provenance-artifact registration, an oraclemcp-db API-baseline refresh, and a Windows durable-state feature gate. A pre-tag dry run would have surfaced all of them ONCE, together, off the tag path.

WHAT: add a workflow_dispatch 'release rehearsal' that runs the exact tag-pipeline gates against a candidate SHA on a branch, before any tag is pushed. Must cover the three that actually bit: the 7-target build INCLUDING the binary-size check, 'cargo package --workspace' tarball verification, and the full release-gates job. This is the server analogue of the driver's RQ prep mode (sec.25.5).

MUST NOT: publish, tag, create a GitHub release, push to GHCR, or touch the MCP registry. Rehearsal is read-only with respect to every outward-facing surface; it exercises gates only. workflow_dispatch means it never fires on push.

SECOND HALF (sec.29.3, same reasoning as sec.27.2 C10 'no silent cap'): make the binary-size budget a RATCHETED warn-then-fail value with headroom rather than a hard cliff that silently goes stale as the binary grows. release.yml already carries a binary_warn_bytes ratchet comment near the build matrix; reconcile with that rather than adding a second mechanism.

ACCEPTANCE: actionlint clean; a dispatch on a non-tag branch completes the build matrix, package verification, and release gates; no publish or tag step is reachable from the rehearsal path; the size budget reports a warn band before it fails.
close_reason: ALREADY IMPLEMENTED — verified, not rebuilt. release.yml carries the §29.3 pre-tag rehearsal (on: [push, workflow_dispatch]; release/publish-crates/docker/publish-mcp-registry each tag-gated, verify-mcp-registry skipped transitively via needs) and the §27.2 C10 size ratchet (::warning:: above binary_warn_bytes, exit 1 above binary_limit_bytes). LIMITS: static verification only, no rehearsal dispatched (outward-facing, unauthorized); actionlint absent on this host so that acceptance criterion was NOT executed. Follow-up oraclemcp-m4kua tracks a guard test so the tag gating cannot silently regress. comments.author: durakovic
comments.text:
VERIFIED DONE, close BLOCKED on tracker semantics -- not on the work. Evidence is committed at tests/artifacts/evidence/closes/oraclemcp-yi5bu.json and the guarded close passes its own preflight ('pre-close: PASS ... evidence landed at HEAD and in-scope paths clean'), then refuses:

  refusing open non-parent dependencies: oraclemcp-eng-program-bp8ia.3(discovered-from:in_progress)

scripts/bead_tracker_guard.sh treats ANY open dependency whose type is not parent-child as a real blocker (lines 101-121). discovered-from is provenance -- 'I found this while working on X' -- not sequencing; a follow-up filed from an open epic is precisely the thing that should be closeable before its parent. As written, every bead filed with --deps discovered-from:<open parent> is unclosable until the parent closes, which inverts the intended relationship and will hit the whole swarm (the orchestrator has instructed all panes to file that way).

I did NOT force past the guard and did NOT retype the dependency to make the gate pass -- rewriting a provenance edge to satisfy a check is exactly what the check exists to prevent. Filed the semantics question separately.

FINDINGS (both halves already shipped in .github/workflows/release.yml): on: resolves to [push, workflow_dispatch]; release / publish-crates / docker / publish-mcp-registry each carry startsWith(github.ref,'refs/tags/'); verify-mcp-registry is skipped transitively via needs. The size ratchet emits ::warning:: above binary_warn_bytes and exits 1 above binary_limit_bytes. Static verification only -- no rehearsal dispatched, and actionlint is absent on this host so that acceptance criterion was not executed.

title: Make profiles.pool runtime behavior honest and session-safe
description:
## Current gap

`[profiles.pool]` is documented and parsed by `oraclemcp-config`, and `oraclemcp-db` contains `OraclePool` plus `PoolSettings`. The shipping binary still opens a single `RustOracleConnection` in `try_open_connection`, so profile pool settings are currently misleading: operators can configure them, `oracle_list_profiles` can report the timeout metadata, but the server does not use a profile-backed pool.

## Required change

Make `[profiles.pool]` honest and functional, or deliberately remove/hide it from the user-facing profile contract until it can be used safely.

The preferred implementation is to wire the existing pool into the server connection strategy without breaking session-affinity-sensitive workflows.

## Design constraints

- Do not break session features that require affinity: transactions, savepoints, temp tables, package globals, DBMS_OUTPUT, login setup, and profile session identity.
- If only read-only stateless tools can safely use the pool, make that split explicit in code and docs.
- If the current `OracleConnection` trait is the wrong abstraction for `OraclePool`, add a narrow adapter rather than pretending pooled and leased sessions have identical semantics.
- Admission control should align with pool `max_size` instead of allowing unbounded agent concurrency to starve the pool.

## Acceptance criteria

- Either `[profiles.pool]` is wired into runtime behavior and verified, or profile docs/metadata stop claiming it is usable.
- Tests prove configured pool settings are converted to `PoolSettings` and used by the connection factory.
- Tests cover max/min/acquire-timeout validation and the default values.
- Tests or docs prove session-affinity-sensitive operations still run on one session or are explicitly excluded from pooled execution.
- `oracle_connection_info`/doctor output should make the active connection strategy clear without leaking connection details.
close_reason: Implemented hybrid_pool runtime strategy for profiles.pool, added routing/tests/docs/doctor reporting, and passed workspace gates plus single-bead compliance audit.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Close evidence: profiles.pool now maps through oraclemcp-config -> PoolSettings -> ResolvedProfile.pool_settings; serve opens a pinned session plus optional OraclePool-backed stateless metadata connection. Dispatcher routes metadata/catalog/source tools to the stateless connection and keeps oracle_query, sample rows, CLOB reads, write/DDL paths, transactions, and DBMS_OUTPUT on the pinned session. OraclePool implements OracleConnection only for query/describe/ping and fails execute/commit/rollback explicitly. oracle_connection_info and doctor report single_session/hybrid_pool/stateless_metadata_pool strategy without connection secrets. Docs and robot docs describe the split and statement_cache_size honesty. Verification: cargo fmt --all -- --check; git diff --check; cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo deny check; cargo test --workspace; single-bead audit 2026-06-18T09-02-01Z scored 1000/1000. Opt-in live target cargo test -p oraclemcp-db --features live-xe --test live_oracle -- --nocapture passed harness but skipped DB assertions because current Oracle credentials fail ORA-01017.

title: SEC-6 regression: OAuth WWW-Authenticate leaks why a token was rejected (auth oracle)
description:
381c4703 'fix(auth): make OAuth token failures actionable' added token_error_description() at crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:529, rendered into the WWW-Authenticate header at oauth_rs.rs:238-239. An UNAUTHENTICATED caller now receives a distinct reason per failure mode:

  Missing              -> 'Bearer access token is required'
  Malformed            -> 'Token is malformed'
  BadSignature         -> 'Token signature did not verify'
  Expired              -> 'Token has expired'
  MissingRequiredClaim -> names the exact claim ('Required RFC 9068 claim iss is missing', and per-claim variants for sub/client_id/jti/iat/exp)
  UnsupportedAlg       -> 'JWT alg is not supported by this resource server'

That is an authentication oracle. Probing with crafted tokens now reveals: whether the token parsed at all; whether the SIGNATURE verified as distinct from whether it EXPIRED (which tells an attacker a captured token is genuine but stale, or that a forgery is structurally correct and only the signature is wrong); the exact set of required claims, by enumeration; and which algorithms the resource server accepts. The signature-vs-expiry distinction is the most valuable of these to an attacker and the least necessary to a legitimate client.

This conflicts with SEC-6 (uniform auth errors) from PLAN §4-RS, which exists precisely to stop an unauthenticated party learning why their credential was refused.

HOW IT SURFACED: it is failing CI, not passing silently. crates/oraclemcp/tests/e2e_http_oauth.rs::binary_http_oauth_rejects_missing_invalid_and_insufficient_tokens asserts the header is exactly error="invalid_token" with NO error_description, and it fails on both Linux (cargo test job) and the Windows lane. THE TEST IS RIGHT AND THE CODE IS WRONG. Do not 'fix' this by updating the test to accept the description — that would bless the leak and is the single wrong outcome available here.

THE TENSION IS REAL, so resolve it rather than picking a side: B2a+B2b legitimately asks to make token errors diagnosable. Diagnosability belongs to the OPERATOR, not to the anonymous caller. The correct shape is a uniform client-facing response (error="invalid_token", no discriminating description) with the specific TokenError reason recorded in the audit/security trail where an operator can read it. That satisfies both requirements instead of trading one for the other.

ACCEPTANCE:
1. Anonymous callers receive an identical WWW-Authenticate challenge regardless of WHY the token failed. A test must assert that two different failure modes (e.g. BadSignature vs Expired) produce byte-identical headers.
2. The specific reason is still recoverable by an operator from the audit/security trail, with a test proving it is recorded.
3. The existing e2e assertion stays as written; it is the contract.
4. If a description is kept for the Missing case only (no token presented leaks nothing, since the caller already knows), state that reasoning explicitly rather than leaving it implicit.

Found by the orchestrator on 2026-07-21 while triaging why oraclemcp CI stayed red after the clippy P0 cleared.
